Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional & Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: Which of the following best describes the Jennings test for establishing a constitutional convention?

options.A: A convention requires three elements: precedents, belief of being bound, and a reason for the rule
options.B: A convention exists if the government has acted in accordance with it on at least two occasions
options.C: A convention is any practice that the courts decide has become law through usage
options.D: A convention can only be established by statute or in works of authority

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: Option A is correct — the Jennings test (from Sir Ivor Jennings' 'The Law and the Constitution') requires: (1) What are the precedents? (2) Did the actors believe themselves bound? (3) Is there a reason for the rule? (FLK1-CAL-004). B is incorrect because the Jennings test requires more than just repetition; it requires evidence of subjective belief in obligation and a reasoned basis. Option C is incorrect — conventions remain non-legal even if courts recognise them; they do not become law through usage. Option D is incorrect — conventions are distinct from statutory rules and may not appear in works of authority.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional & Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.subtopic: Nature and sources of the constitution
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: What is the key difference between the UK constitution being 'uncodified' rather than 'unwritten'?

options.B: Uncodified means that most of the constitution is recorded on paper, whereas unwritten means it relies entirely on oral tradition
options.A: Uncodified means the constitution is not contained in a single legal document of higher status, although many sources are written
options.C: Uncodified means the constitution cannot be changed, whereas unwritten means it can be amended by Act of Parliament
options.D: Uncodified refers only to common law constitutional principles, whereas unwritten includes statutory sources

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: Option A is correct. The UK constitution is better described as uncodified — it lacks a single codified document of higher legal status — but many sources are written (statutes like HRA 1998, case law, authoritative works) (FLK1-CAL-052). B is incorrect; 'unwritten' does not mean oral — many constitutional sources are written. Option C is incorrect — both uncodified and written constitutions can be changed; the uncodified status means change is easier (by ordinary Act). Option D is incorrect — uncodified encompasses all sources; it is not a distinction between types of source.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional & Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: A recent Act of Parliament provides: 'Notwithstanding any other Act, the government shall not negotiate any international trade agreement without a referendum.' Six months later, Parliament passes the 'Trade Facilitation Act 2026', which authorises the government to negotiate and ratify trade agreements at its discretion, with no mention of referenda. Which principle of constitutional law best explains the courts' likely response?

options.B: The courts would strike down the 2026 Act because it violates the rule of law by removing a fundamental safeguard
options.A: The courts would apply the principle of implied repeal — later inconsistent legislation impliedly repeals earlier legislation unless the latter is a constitutional statute
options.C: The courts would refuse to interfere because both Acts are supremely valid and there is no hierarchical relationship between them
options.D: The courts would apply the principle of express repeal — the 2026 Act is only valid if it explicitly states it repeals the earlier Act

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: Option A is correct. This scenario tests the doctrine of parliamentary sovereignty and implied repeal. If the earlier Act is not a constitutional statute, a later inconsistent Act will impliedly repeal it — even without express words. The courts cannot strike down either Act (rejecting A). There is an implied repeal doctrine that applies to ordinary statutes (C is therefore incomplete/incorrect in suggesting no hierarchy). Option D is wrong because implied repeal does not require express words — that is precisely what distinguishes it from express repeal (FLK1-CAL-002, FLK1-CAL-010).

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional & Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: The Prime Minister announces that Parliament will be prorogued for five weeks to allow the government to prepare its legislative programme, without seeking parliamentary approval. A claimant argues the prorogation is unlawful. The government contends it acted within the royal prerogative. Which of the following best describes the nature of the royal prerogative?

options.A: Residual common law powers of the Crown not derived from statute, subject to judicial review where they affect legal rights.
options.B: Powers vested in the Crown that were once unlimited but are now entirely superseded by statute.
options.C: Powers that the Monarch personally exercises at her own discretion, independently of ministerial advice.
options.D: Powers that exist only where no statute has expressly prohibited their exercise.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: Option A is correct. The royal prerogative comprises residual common law powers of the Crown that are not derived from statute. They include the power to prorogue Parliament, appoint ministers, declare war, and grant mercy. Following Miller (No.2) [2019], prerogative powers are subject to judicial review where they affect legal rights or constitutional principles — proroguing Parliament to frustrate parliamentary scrutiny was held unlawful. Option B is wrong: the prerogative has not been entirely superseded; many prerogative powers remain in force until abolished by statute. Option C is wrong: the Monarch acts on ministerial advice in all but the most exceptional cases. Option D is wrong: the prerogative may exist even where statute touches the same area, unless the statute expressly or impliedly removes it (A-G v De Keyser's Royal Hotel [1920]).

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional & Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: The Home Secretary proposes to use the royal prerogative to suspend habeas corpus in a particular region to combat terrorism. A prisoner seeks judicial review. Which statement best describes the court's approach under modern law on justiciability of prerogative powers?

options.B: The court will refuse to hear the case because the prerogative is fundamentally non-justiciable and immune from review
options.A: The court will review the decision under the subject-matter test: some prerogative decisions (e.g. national security, foreign affairs) are non-justiciable, while others (e.g. decisions affecting individual rights like habeas corpus) are justiciable
options.C: The court will strike down the prerogative power because it conflicts with the HRA 1998
options.D: The court will allow the prerogative but require the Home Secretary to secure parliamentary approval first

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: Option A is correct. The modern position, established in CCSU v Minister for Civil Service [1985], is that prerogative powers are justiciable subject to a subject-matter test: some areas (national security, foreign affairs, defence) are unsuitable for judicial review due to their nature, while others affecting individual rights (like suspension of habeas corpus) are reviewable (FLK1-CAL-014, FLK1-CAL-CASE-009). B is outdated and too absolute. Option C is incorrect — the court reviews, it does not routinely strike down prerogatives; it applies proportionality under HRA s.6 but does not invalidate the power itself. Option D is incorrect — the court does not impose a requirement to seek parliamentary approval through judicial review; that is a matter of convention.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional & Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.subtopic: Sections 3 and 4: interpretation and declaration of incompatibility
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: Under the Human Rights Act 1998, which of the following accurately describes the duty under section 3?

options.A: Section 3 requires courts to read and give effect to legislation in a way that is compatible with Convention rights, so far as it is possible to do so
options.B: Section 3 allows courts to strike down legislation that is incompatible with Convention rights
options.C: Section 3 permits courts to set aside legislation only by making a declaration of incompatibility, which binds no one
options.D: Section 3 applies only to common law and does not apply to statutory interpretation

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: Option A is correct. Section 3(1) of the HRA 1998 requires courts to read and give effect to legislation in a way compatible with Convention rights, 'so far as it is possible to do so' (FLK1-CAL-028, Ghaidan v Godin-Mendoza [2004]). This is a strong interpretive duty, but it has limits — if it would require departing from fundamental features of the legislation, it may not be possible. B is incorrect — s.3 does not allow courts to strike down legislation; that power is limited by parliamentary sovereignty. Option C confuses s.3 with s.4; s.3 is interpretation, s.4 is declaration of incompatibility. Option D is incorrect — s.3 applies to all legislation, not just common law.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional & Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.subtopic: Sections 3 and 4: interpretation and declaration of incompatibility
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: A statute defines 'marriage' as the union of a man and a woman. Under the HRA 1998, a transgender woman challenges this definition. The courts determine that a s.3 interpretation to read the statute as including same-sex marriages would require reading words into the statute that are not there. Which statement best describes the court's next step?

options.C: The courts must rewrite the statute to conform to Convention rights, even if this requires going beyond s.3 limits
options.A: The courts must strike down the statute because it violates the right to marry under Art 12 ECHR
options.B: The courts have exhausted their duty under s.3 and may make a declaration of incompatibility under s.4, which binds the courts but does not invalidate the statute
options.D: The courts must declare the statute void under the HRA and the matter is conclusively resolved

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: Option B is correct. When s.3 interpretation is not possible (because it would depart from fundamental features), courts may make a declaration of incompatibility under s.4 (FLK1-CAL-029, Bellinger v Bellinger [2003]). This declaration does not invalidate the statute but flags the incompatibility and prompts executive/legislative review. Option A is incorrect — courts cannot strike down primary legislation; the remedy is s.4 declaration. Option C is incorrect — courts cannot simply rewrite statutes; s.3 and s.4 set the limits. Option D is incorrect — a s.4 declaration does not void the statute; it is declaratory only.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional & Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: Under section 6 of the HRA 1998, which of the following correctly states what makes an action unlawful?

options.B: An action by a public authority is unlawful if it is incompatible with a Convention right, subject to exceptions in the ECHR itself and primary legislation
options.A: An action by a public authority is unlawful if it breaches any common law principle, regardless of Convention rights
options.C: An action by a public authority is unlawful only if Parliament has not authorised the action in statute
options.D: An action by a public authority is unlawful if a court declares it to be so after a full trial on the merits

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: Option B is correct. Section 6(1) of the HRA 1998 makes it unlawful for a public authority to act in a way that is incompatible with a Convention right, subject to exceptions (s.6(2) — where primary legislation requires the incompatibility) (FLK1-CAL-031). Option A is incorrect — s.6 is specific to Convention rights, not all common law. Option C is incorrect — statutory authorisation does not justify breaches of Convention rights (though s.6(2) provides a defence for compliance with primary legislation). Option D is incorrect — unlawfulness exists under s.6 itself; it does not require a separate declaration.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional & Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: A prison governor restricts a prisoner's access to legal correspondence on grounds of security. The prisoner argues this breaches the right to a fair trial (Art 6 ECHR). Under the HRA 1998, how would a court assess this restriction?

options.C: The court would apply a proportionality assessment: whether the restriction pursues a legitimate aim, is rationally connected to it, and is not excessive (de Freitas test)
options.A: The court would uphold the restriction automatically because national security is mentioned in the statute
options.B: The court would strike down the restriction because Art 6 is an absolute right that cannot be interfered with
options.D: The court would defer entirely to the prison governor's decision because prison management is a matter of executive discretion

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: Option C is correct. Art 6 is engaged here (right to a fair trial, including access to legal advice), and while security concerns may be legitimate, the court applies proportionality review under the de Freitas test: is there a legitimate aim, rational connection, and proportionality? (FLK1-CAL-033). This reflects how qualified rights are assessed under HRA s.6 and JR principles. Option A is incorrect — national security does not automatically justify breaches. Option B is incorrect — Art 6 is not absolute; it can be subject to restrictions if proportionate. Option D is incorrect — courts will review the decision for proportionality; deference is not abdication of review.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional & Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.subtopic: Illegality: fettering of discretion and improper purpose
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: A local authority policy provides: 'The housing committee shall refuse all applications for housing benefit from applicants whose income exceeds £30,000 per annum, without considering the circumstances of any individual case.' A resident earning £32,000 applies for housing benefit due to a serious illness that prevents work. The committee refuses without considering circumstances. Which principle of JR has been breached?

options.B: Illegality — the committee has fettered its discretion by adopting a blanket policy that excludes consideration of individual cases
options.A: Procedural impropriety — the committee failed to provide a fair hearing
options.C: Irrationality — the committee's decision is so unreasonable that no reasonable authority would reach it
options.D: Legitimate expectation — the resident had a legitimate expectation of sympathetic consideration

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: Option B is correct. The committee has fettered its discretion by adopting an inflexible policy that prevents consideration of individual circumstances (FLK1-CAL-037, R v SSHD ex p Venables [1997]). Discretion must always be exercised by reference to the circumstances of the case; a blanket policy that excludes individual consideration is ultra vires. Option A is incorrect — this is not primarily about procedure but about the exercise of discretion itself. Option C (irrationality) might also apply, but the core breach is fettering of discretion. Option D is incorrect — legitimate expectation applies to procedural rights or substantive entitlements represented as policy, not to sympathetic consideration as such.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional & Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: A local authority grants planning permission for a leisure facility on public land. Evidence at judicial review reveals that the real reason for approval was to improve the reputational standing of the council leader (who owns a nearby property). The stated reason was 'to promote local employment and community benefit.' The applicant seeks quashing. Which principle of illegality applies most directly?

options.C: Acting for an improper purpose — the actual purpose (reputation) differs fundamentally from the statutory purpose (planning and community benefit)
options.A: Fettering of discretion — the authority failed to consider relevant circumstances
options.B: Taking into account irrelevant considerations — the council considered the reputation of the leader
options.D: Procedural impropriety — the council failed to consult properly before deciding

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: Option C is correct. The council has acted for an improper purpose — the actual purpose (personal reputation of the leader) is inconsistent with the statutory purposes of planning law (FLK1-CAL-038, Wheeler v Leicester CC [1985]). Improper purpose is a sub-category of illegality. Option A is incorrect — fettering of discretion is about failing to exercise discretion, not about motivations. Option B is incorrect — the council considered a relevant factor (employment/community benefit in the stated reason), but the actual motivation was improper. Option D is incorrect — consultation is about procedure; the breach here is substantive (purpose).

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional & Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: In judicial review, what does 'Wednesbury unreasonableness' require the claimant to prove?

options.A: That the decision-maker made a factual error that changed the outcome
options.B: That the decision is wrong or that the claimant disagrees with it
options.C: That the decision is so unreasonable that no reasonable decision-maker could have reached it; the decision must be irrational, not merely wrong
options.D: That the decision fails to give proper weight to relevant considerations

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: Option C is correct. Wednesbury unreasonableness (from Associated Provincial Picture Houses v Wednesbury Corp [1948]) is a high threshold: the decision must be so irrational that no reasonable authority could reach it (FLK1-CAL-039). It is not enough to show the claimant disagrees or the decision is wrong. Option A is incorrect — mere disagreement is insufficient. Option B is incorrect — a factual error might be an error of fact (distinct from Wednesbury), but Wednesbury is about irrationality of process/reasoning. Option D is incorrect — that describes a different ground (taking into account relevant/irrelevant considerations), not Wednesbury unreasonableness itself.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional & Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: A prison governor imposes a blanket ban on all visits to the prison for one month as punishment for a single violent incident by one prisoner. The ban affects hundreds of prisoners and their families, many of whom had no involvement in the incident. A prisoner's mother seeks judicial review. What is the modern approach of English courts?

options.C: The court applies proportionality review: the ban must be rational and necessary, and the disadvantage to prisoners must be proportionate to the legitimate aim
options.A: The court applies the test of Wednesbury unreasonableness — the ban is only quashed if no reasonable governor could impose it
options.B: The court cannot review the proportionality of disciplinary decisions; this is a matter of prison management discretion
options.D: The court automatically quashes any ban lasting more than one week as arbitrary

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: Option C is correct. Modern case law (R v MOD ex p Smith [1996]) shows that where fundamental rights are engaged (family life, liberty), courts now apply proportionality review rather than mere Wednesbury unreasonableness (FLK1-CAL-040, FLK1-CAL-CASE-018). The measure must be rationally connected to the aim and not excessive. This reflects HRA 1998 principles. Option A is too lenient; Wednesbury alone may not capture the disproportionality. Option B is incorrect — courts will review even executive decisions on proportionality grounds. Option D is incorrect — there is no bright-line rule about duration; it is about proportionality to the aim.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional & Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.subtopic: Procedural impropriety: bias and fair hearing
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: A planning inspector is appointed to hear an application by Wharton Magna Developments Ltd. It emerges that six months earlier the inspector gave an after-dinner speech at an event organised by the local residents' association that has formally objected to the development. The developer applies to quash the decision on grounds of apparent bias. Which test applies to determine whether the inspector should be disqualified?

options.A: The inspector is biased if the developer subjectively believes the inspector was not impartial.
options.B: The inspector must be disqualified only if they personally benefited from the outcome.
options.C: The inspector must be disqualified if a fair-minded observer, knowing all the relevant facts, would conclude there was a real possibility of bias.
options.D: The inspector is biased only if they previously expressed a settled view contrary to the developer's position.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: Option C is correct. The fair-minded observer test, established in Porter v Magill [2001] and affirmed in Helow v SSHD [2008], asks whether a fair-minded and informed observer, having considered all the relevant facts, would conclude there was a real possibility of bias. The test is objective — it does not depend on whether the developer actually believes the inspector was biased (ruling out Option A), nor on personal financial benefit (ruling out B). Option D is wrong: prior expressed views are evidence relevant to the test, but the test is not confined to cases where a settled view was previously expressed. On the facts, the inspector's association with the objectors would be a relevant fact for the fair-minded observer to consider.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional & Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.subtopic: Procedural impropriety: natural justice and fair hearing
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: A student is expelled from university after a disciplinary hearing. The student was not informed of the allegations in advance, was not given an opportunity to hear the evidence against them, and was not allowed to present a defence. The university argues that the decision is a matter of contract law, not public law, and therefore JR does not apply. Which statement best describes the court's likely approach?

options.B: The court would apply JR principles, requiring the university to comply with natural justice (notice, hearing, fair consideration), because the university is exercising a quasi-judicial power affecting the student's rights
options.A: The court would refuse to hear the claim because the university's internal disciplinary procedures are a matter of private law only
options.C: The court would require the university to prove the student was guilty beyond reasonable doubt, as in criminal proceedings
options.D: The court would uphold the expulsion if the university acted in accordance with its own published procedures, even if those procedures are unfair

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: Option B is correct. Although the relationship may be contractual, universities are public authorities subject to JR when exercising powers affecting fundamental rights. Natural justice requires notice, an opportunity to be heard, and fair consideration (FLK1-CAL-043). Option A is incorrect — the fact that a relationship is contractual does not exclude JR entirely; quasi-judicial functions can be reviewed. Option C is incorrect — criminal standards of proof do not apply to disciplinary proceedings; the test is balance of probabilities. Option D is incorrect — even if the university followed its own procedures, if those procedures breach natural justice, the decision may be quashed.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional & Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: What is the distinction between procedural and substantive legitimate expectations in judicial review?

options.C: Procedural expectations require the authority to follow a fair process; substantive expectations require the authority to grant a specific benefit or maintain a particular policy
options.A: Procedural expectations relate to the substance of a benefit, while substantive expectations relate to the process by which a decision is made
options.B: Procedural legitimate expectations are always enforceable, whereas substantive legitimate expectations are never enforceable
options.D: Procedural expectations apply only to statutory authorities, while substantive expectations apply to common law powers

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: Option C is correct. Procedural legitimate expectation requires the authority to follow a fair process (e.g. a hearing before withdrawal of a benefit); substantive legitimate expectation requires the authority to grant a specific benefit or policy if it has represented it will do so (FLK1-CAL-044, R v NI Council ex p Coughlan [2001]). Option A reverses the definitions. Option B is incorrect — substantive legitimate expectations can be enforceable in appropriate circumstances (when they do not prevent the authority from exercising discretion). Option D is incorrect — both types apply to public authorities generally.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional & Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: A local authority publishes a planning policy: 'Retirement homes for over-60s will ordinarily be approved in the Green Belt, provided they meet health and safety standards.' A developer submits a planning application for a retirement home that meets all health and safety standards. The authority's planning committee rejects the application, stating that it has changed its policy. The developer argues legitimate expectation. Which statement best reflects the modern law?

options.D: The developer's claim fails because the authority always retains discretion to change its policies
options.A: The developer has no claim because legitimate expectations cannot arise from published planning policies
options.B: The developer has a procedural legitimate expectation to a fair hearing, but no substantive entitlement to approval under the old policy
options.C: The developer may have a substantive legitimate expectation that the authority will follow its published policy, and the authority may be required to justify the change or grant approval to avoid unfairness

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: Option C is correct. Published policies can generate substantive legitimate expectations. The authority is not strictly bound, but if it changes policy mid-application, it must justify the change or treat the applicant fairly (e.g. by granting the benefit or providing a transition). The test is whether there is unfairness, not absolute entitlement (FLK1-CAL-044, Bhatt Murphy [2008]). Option A is incorrect — policies do generate expectations. Option B is incorrect — only a procedural expectation understates the position; substantive expectations are possible. Option D is incorrect — while the authority has discretion, this does not negate legitimate expectation; it requires fair exercise of discretion.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional & Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: Under the Senior Courts Act 1981 section 31 and CPR Part 54, what is required to establish 'standing' (or 'sufficient interest') in a judicial review claim?

options.A: The claimant must be the only person affected by the decision
options.B: The claimant must show they have suffered a direct financial loss as a result of the decision
options.C: The claimant must show they have a sufficient interest in the matter to which the claim relates — a flexible test depending on the circumstances
options.D: The claimant must have brought the claim within one week of the decision

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: Option C is correct. Standing is established by showing 'sufficient interest' in the matter (FLK1-CAL-045). This is a flexible test that depends on the nature of the decision and the claimant's relationship to it. It can include direct personal interests, interests as a member of the public affected by the decision, and campaigning/representative interests. Option A is incorrect — direct financial loss is not required; the test is broader. Option B is incorrect — multiple claimants may have standing. Option D is incorrect — that describes a time limit, not standing.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional & Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: A claimant becomes aware of an unlawful decision by a local authority to grant planning permission for a development in their neighbourhood. The claimant is very busy and does not file a claim until 4 months after the decision. The authority argues that the claim should be struck out for delay. Under CPR Part 54, what is the legal position?

options.D: The claim may proceed if the claimant pays compensation to the authority for the delay
options.A: The claim must be heard because the claimant has a right to review unlawful decisions regardless of time
options.B: The claim must be struck out because the three-month time limit is absolute and cannot be extended
options.C: The claim must be filed within three months of the date when the claimant knew or ought to have known of the decision; failure to comply means the court may refuse permission unless the claimant can show good reason for delay

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: Option C is correct. The three-month time limit (known as 'promptly') is set by CPR Part 54 (FLK1-CAL-046). The court may refuse permission for late claims unless there is good reason for the delay and no prejudice to others. The test is discretionary, not absolute. Option A is incorrect — time limits apply even to unlawful decisions. Option B is incorrect — the limit is not absolute; it can be extended in appropriate circumstances. Option D is incorrect — compensation is not a mechanism for overcoming time limits in JR.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional & Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: After successful judicial review, a planning officer has quashed an unlawful planning permission. However, the authority refuses to reconsider the application or take any further action, citing pressure of work. The claimant seeks to compel reconsideration. Which remedy would be most appropriate?

options.C: A declaration that the original decision was unlawful
options.A: An injunction preventing the authority from issuing further planning permissions
options.B: A mandatory order requiring the authority to reconsider the application
options.D: Damages for the delay in reconsideration

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: Option B is correct. A mandatory order (formerly called an order of mandamus) compels a public authority to perform a public duty (FLK1-CAL-047). Here, the authority has a duty to reconsider after quashing. Option A is incorrect — an injunction is not the appropriate remedy for compelling performance of a specific duty. Option C is incorrect — a declaration has already been made (the permission was quashed). Option D is incorrect — damages are available under HRA s.8 if Convention rights are breached, but not automatically in JR.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional & Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: Parliament passes the Environmental Licences (Override) Act 2025, which expressly provides that its provisions prevail over any earlier Act of Parliament to the contrary. An environmental law organisation argues that the 2025 Act cannot override entrenched protections in the Climate Change Act 2008. According to Dicey's classical formulation of parliamentary sovereignty, which statement is correct?

options.A: Parliament has power to make or unmake any law whatever; no body can override an Act of Parliament, and no Parliament can bind its successors.
options.B: Parliament may entrench legislation so that later Parliaments cannot repeal it without a special procedure.
options.C: Parliament's power is limited by the rule of law and fundamental rights that courts can enforce.
options.D: Parliament must consult the courts before passing legislation that conflicts with existing statutes.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: Option A is correct. Dicey's classical formulation identifies three limbs: (1) Parliament may legislate on any subject matter; (2) no Parliament can bind its successors (so the 2025 Act can override the 2008 Act despite its terms); (3) no court may override Parliament's legislation. Applying Dicey strictly, the 2025 Act would override the 2008 Act because a later Parliament cannot be bound by an earlier one — the organisation's argument fails. Option B is wrong: under Dicey's formulation entrenchment is constitutionally impossible. Option C describes a modified or rights-based constitutionalism, not Dicey's classical view. Option D is wrong: Parliament needs no judicial approval before legislating.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional & Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: Parliament passes Statute A, which expressly provides: 'This Act protects the right to a fair trial and may only be repealed by express words in a later Act.' Twenty years later, Parliament passes Statute B, which contains comprehensive new court procedures but makes no mention of the fair trial protections in Statute A. Does Statute B impliedly repeal the relevant provisions of Statute A?

options.D: No, because Statute A contains an entrenchment clause that binds Parliament
options.A: Yes, because all Acts of Parliament are equal and Statute B, being later, automatically repeal Statute A
options.B: No, because Statute A is a constitutional statute and cannot be impliedly repealed; if Parliament intended to repeal it, it must use express words
options.C: Yes, unless the court finds that Statute A is 'more important' than Statute B

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: Option B is correct. Although Statute A's own entrenchment clause is not binding (Parliament cannot bind itself), the fact that it is a constitutional statute means the common law doctrine against implied repeal applies (Thoburn v Sunderland [2002]) (FLK1-CAL-009). Express words are required to repeal it. Option A is incorrect — this ignores the distinction between constitutional and ordinary statutes. Option C is incorrect — entrenchment clauses are not binding in the UK, but the common law doctrine still applies. Option D is incorrect — the court does not rank statutes by 'importance'; it applies a rule based on their constitutional character.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional & Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: In R (Miller) v Secretary of State for Exiting the EU [2017], the Supreme Court held that the government could not trigger Article 50 without an Act of Parliament. Which constitutional principles did the court rely upon, and does it represent a limitation on parliamentary sovereignty?

options.D: The court relied on common law requiring consent of the Scottish Parliament; this contradicts Dicey's theory of sovereignty
options.A: The court held that the EU itself had authority to prevent UK withdrawal; this is a limitation on sovereignty because it subordinates Parliament to EU law
options.B: The court struck down the prerogative action as unconstitutional; this represents a fundamental change to the doctrine of parliamentary sovereignty
options.C: The court relied on the principle of legality to require statutory authority for the prerogative action; this does not limit parliamentary sovereignty because Parliament could authorize the action by statute

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: Option C is correct. Miller [2017] applied the principle of legality: the prerogative cannot be used to change domestic law without statutory authority (FLK1-CAL-011, FLK1-CAL-CASE-012). This does not limit parliamentary sovereignty; it shows sovereignty working through Parliament. If Parliament enacted the trigger, the prerogative would be valid. Option A is incorrect — the court did not subordinate UK law to EU law; it required UK statutory authority. Option B is incorrect — the prerogative action was not deemed unconstitutional; it was simply not authorized by law. Option D is incorrect — while the Sewel convention was discussed, the court's main holding did not turn on it, and it did not override parliamentary sovereignty.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional & Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: A statute gives a minister power to make decisions 'in the interests of public health.' The minister considers not only public health but also wider government economic policy. A campaigner challenges the decision as unlawful, arguing the minister considered irrelevant economic considerations. Under modern principles of JR, which statement best captures the court's likely approach?

options.C: The court would quash the decision because economic policy is never relevant to health decisions
options.A: The court would refuse to review the decision because the minister has discretion to consider all factors
options.B: The court would apply a test of proportionality: the minister is entitled to balance factors, but only if the decision is rationally connected to the statutory purpose and not disproportionate to the stated aim
options.D: The court would apply the test from Associated Provincial Picture Houses v Wednesbury Corp — the decision is only quashed if it is so irrational that no reasonable minister could reach it

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: Option B is correct. Modern law applies a proportionality test to discretionary decisions, especially where fundamental rights or important interests are engaged (FLK1-CAL-040). The minister can consider multiple factors, but the decision must be rationally connected to the statutory purpose and the balance must be proportionate. Option A is incorrect — discretion is not unlimited; it must be exercised for proper purposes and with relevant considerations. Option C is incorrect — economic considerations may be relevant to health, depending on context. Option D is incorrect — while Wednesbury still applies to some decisions, proportionality is now the preferred test where justified.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional & Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: A statute defines 'parent' as 'a person who has the biological capacity to bear children.' A same-sex couple, who have adopted children, argue this definition violates their right to family life (Art 8 ECHR) and equal treatment (Art 14 ECHR). Can a court cure the incompatibility through s.3 interpretation? Which factor is most important in making this choice?

options.C: The court can read the definition to include 'adoptive parent' under s.3, as this interpretation is compatible with the statutory scheme and achieves Convention compliance
options.A: The court must always prefer s.3 interpretation, even if it requires reading words into the statute that are not there
options.B: The court must make a s.4 declaration because same-sex couples are not biological parents and cannot meet the literal words of the statute
options.D: The court can proceed under s.3 only if Parliament explicitly consents to the reinterpretation

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: Option C is correct. Section 3 allows a broad interpretation where it does not fundamentally depart from the statutory purpose. If 'parent' can be read as including adoptive parents (consistent with the overall purpose of protecting family units), s.3 can be applied (FLK1-CAL-028, Ghaidan v Godin-Mendoza [2004]). This is the preferred approach to achieve Convention compliance. Option A is incorrect — s.3 has limits; it cannot be used if it departs from fundamental features. Option B is incorrect — statutory language can be read more broadly if it achieves Convention compatibility. Option D is incorrect — Parliament's consent is not required; the court has the power to interpret.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional & Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: A prison service policy states: 'Prisoners serving life sentences may apply for parole review after 25 years; all such applications will receive a full, fair hearing.' A prisoner, after 25 years, applies for parole review. The parole board holds a hearing, but pre-judges the outcome without genuinely considering the prisoner's case. The prisoner argues both procedural impropriety and breach of legitimate expectation. Which statement best describes the overlap?

options.D: The court would dismiss the claim because the parole board has discretion over all parole decisions
options.A: Procedural impropriety and legitimate expectation are identical; the court would apply only one ground
options.B: The prisoner has only a procedural legitimate expectation, not a right to a fair hearing in administrative law
options.C: Procedural impropriety (unfair hearing) and legitimate expectation (entitlement to fair consideration) overlap, and the court may apply both grounds; the breach of either is sufficient to quash and require reconsideration

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: Option C is correct. Procedural impropriety (breach of natural justice/fair hearing) and procedural legitimate expectation (entitlement to fair process) often overlap in practice (FLK1-CAL-042, FLK1-CAL-044). The court may apply both grounds; a breach of either is sufficient to quash and require a fair reconsideration. Option A is incorrect — they are distinct grounds, though they often coincide. Option B is incorrect — administrative law does protect fair hearing rights. Option D is incorrect — discretion must be exercised fairly; it is not absolute.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional & Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: A statute establishes a statutory scheme for approving firefighter pay deals. A separate statute preserves the Crown's prerogative to determine the terms and conditions of civil servants. The government seeks to use the prerogative to override the statutory pay scheme for firefighters. Which principle best explains why this would likely be unlawful?

options.A: All prerogative powers relating to employment are invalid under the HRA 1998
options.B: The government needs parliamentary consent to use the prerogative
options.C: The statute has impliedly limited the prerogative to the extent it is incompatible; the Crown cannot use the prerogative to frustrate or undermine the statutory scheme
options.D: The prerogative is always superior to statute

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: Option C is correct. Where a statute establishes a comprehensive scheme, the prerogative cannot be used to frustrate or undermine it (R v SS Home Dept ex p Fire Brigades Union [1995]) (FLK1-CAL-015). The statute limits the prerogative to the extent of incompatibility. Option A is incorrect — there is no blanket requirement; the principle is about incompatibility with an established statutory scheme. Option B is incorrect — the HRA does not automatically invalidate prerogative employment powers; they must be exercised compatibly with Convention rights. Option D is incorrect — statute is superior to prerogative in cases of conflict.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional & Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.subtopic: Rule of law and the principle of legality
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: What does the 'principle of legality' require in the interpretation of legislation affecting fundamental rights?

options.A: Fundamental rights can only be protected by the HRA 1998, not by common law interpretation
options.B: Courts must strike down any legislation that appears to remove fundamental rights
options.C: Courts must defer to Parliament's stated purpose, even if it removes fundamental rights
options.D: Courts will presume that Parliament did not intend to remove fundamental common law rights unless the statute expresses that intention clearly and explicitly

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: Option D is correct. The principle of legality (R v SSHD ex p Simms [1999], R v SSHD ex p Leech [1994]) holds that courts will not assume Parliament intended to remove fundamental rights unless clear words are used (FLK1-CAL-053). This is a tool of interpretation, not a power to strike down statutes. Option A is incorrect — courts cannot strike down primary legislation. Option B is incorrect — courts do protect rights through interpretation. Option C is incorrect — common law interpretation (the principle of legality) does protect fundamental rights, alongside the HRA 1998.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional & Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.subtopic: Legislative competence and the Sewel convention
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: The Scottish Parliament has exclusively devolved powers to legislate on education. The UK Parliament passes an Act that amends education law in Scotland without securing the consent of the Scottish Parliament (contrary to the Sewel convention). A Scottish education group seeks judicial review of the UK Act. Based on Miller [2017], what would be the court's likely response?

options.D: The court would recognise the Sewel convention as a constitutional principle but would not enforce it as law; the Act would remain valid, though the breach may be significant politically
options.A: The court would require the UK Parliament to obtain Scottish consent before the Act becomes law
options.B: The court would not hear the claim because devolution matters are outside the scope of JR
options.C: The court would quash the UK Act as unconstitutional because it breaches the Sewel convention

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: Option D is correct. R (Miller) v SSHD [2017] confirmed that the Sewel convention is recognized by the courts but is not legally enforceable (FLK1-CAL-049). A breach has political consequences, not legal ones. The Act would remain valid. Option A is incorrect — the court cannot impose a legal requirement to obtain consent; that is a political matter. Option B is incorrect — devolution matters are within the scope of JR, but the enforcement of conventions is not. Option C is incorrect — conventions are not enforceable in law.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional & Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.subtopic: Fettering of discretion and the balance of competing interests
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: A local authority adopts a blanket policy refusing all applications for discretionary rate relief unless applicants first demonstrate 'exceptional hardship'. A small business applies and is turned away at the threshold stage without any consideration of the specific facts of its case. The business seeks judicial review. What is the strongest ground of challenge?

options.A: Illegality — the authority acted outside its statutory powers by adopting any policy on rate relief
options.B: Proportionality — the threshold of 'exceptional hardship' is disproportionate to the regulatory objective
options.C: Irrationality — no reasonable authority would refuse to consider rate relief applications
options.D: Fettering of discretion — the policy, by imposing a threshold that prevents any individual assessment of an applicant's circumstances, unlawfully fetters the authority's statutory discretion; each application must receive genuine individual consideration

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: Fettering of discretion occurs where a public authority adopts a rigid policy that prevents it from exercising an individual judgment in each case, as required by its statutory power. A discretion cannot be surrendered or fettered by policy: R v Port of London Authority ex parte Kynoch [1919]. The authority may adopt a policy to guide its discretion, but must always be willing to consider exceptions and to look at the individual merits of each case. Refusing all applications that do not meet the threshold without any individual assessment is an unlawful fetter. Note: proportionality (option B) is not a freestanding ground of challenge in domestic judicial review for non-EU/ECHR cases.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional & Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: Councillor Stubbs, acting as chair of Wharton Magna Planning Committee, refuses Marcus Bridgewater's planning application on the ground that the development 'would not benefit local businesses'. This reason is not found in planning legislation. On what ground of judicial review is this decision most vulnerable?

options.A: Irrationality — no reasonable committee would reach this conclusion.
options.B: Illegality — the decision-maker has taken into account an irrelevant consideration and/or acted outside the legal powers conferred by the relevant legislation (Council of Civil Service Unions v Minister for the Civil Service [1985]).
options.C: Procedural impropriety — the committee failed to follow its own procedure.
options.D: Proportionality — the refusal is disproportionate to the benefit obtained.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: In CCSU v Minister for the Civil Service [1985] the House of Lords identified three grounds of judicial review: illegality, irrationality (Wednesbury unreasonableness), and procedural impropriety. Illegality encompasses acting outside the scope of legal powers (ultra vires), including taking into account irrelevant considerations or ignoring relevant ones. 'Benefit to local businesses' is not a material planning consideration under the Town and Country Planning Act — it is legally irrelevant and its use renders the decision unlawful on grounds of illegality. Irrationality (A) concerns extreme unreasonableness, not the use of wrong criteria. Procedural impropriety (C) concerns process failures. Proportionality (D) is recognised in HRA/EU contexts but is less straightforwardly applicable here.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional & Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: Which statement correctly describes the Wednesbury unreasonableness ground of judicial review?

options.A: A decision is unlawful if the court disagrees with the outcome.
options.B: A decision is unlawful if it is so unreasonable that no reasonable authority could ever have come to it (Associated Provincial Picture Houses Ltd v Wednesbury Corporation [1948]); the court does not substitute its own view for that of the decision-maker.
options.C: A decision is unlawful if it lacks proportionality.
options.D: A decision is unlawful if the decision-maker failed to give reasons.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: Wednesbury unreasonableness (Associated Provincial Picture Houses v Wednesbury [1948]) is a high threshold: the decision must be so unreasonable that no reasonable authority, properly directing itself on the relevant law and facts, could have reached it. It is not enough for the court to disagree — there must be an extreme or outrageous unreasonableness. This test is deferential to public authorities. Courts have applied a more intensive review in cases involving fundamental rights (R v Ministry of Defence ex parte Smith [1996]). Proportionality (C) is a related but distinct ground. Failure to give reasons (D) is a procedural impropriety point.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional & Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: Marcus Bridgewater applies for a licence from Wharton Magna Council. The council refuses the application without giving him any opportunity to make representations. Which ground of judicial review is engaged?

options.A: Illegality — the council acted outside its powers.
options.B: Procedural impropriety — including breach of natural justice (the right to a fair hearing / audi alteram partem); Bridgewater had a legitimate expectation of being heard before an adverse decision was made.
options.C: Irrationality — no reasonable authority would refuse without hearing the applicant.
options.D: Proportionality — the refusal without hearing is disproportionate.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: Procedural impropriety encompasses two main categories: (i) failure to comply with statutory procedural requirements; and (ii) breach of the rules of natural justice (including the right to a fair hearing — audi alteram partem — and the rule against bias — nemo iudex in causa sua). Where a decision significantly affects an individual's rights or legitimate expectations, natural justice requires that person to be given notice and an opportunity to make representations before the decision is taken (Ridge v Baldwin [1964]). Bridgewater had no opportunity to be heard — this is a procedural impropriety. The other grounds do not directly address the procedural defect.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional & Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: Marcus Bridgewater wishes to seek judicial review of a planning decision made four months ago. He delayed because he was trying to resolve the matter informally. What time limit applies?

options.A: Six years — the standard limitation period applies to judicial review.
options.B: An application for judicial review must be made promptly and in any event within three months of the date when the grounds for the claim first arose (CPR r.54.5); planning cases have a shorter period of six weeks; the court may extend time but delay without good reason will be refused.
options.C: There is no fixed time limit — the court has complete discretion.
options.D: One year — the same as for personal injury claims.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: CPR r.54.5 requires a claim for judicial review to be filed promptly and in any event within three months of the date when the grounds for the claim first arose. For planning cases under the Town and Country Planning Act 1990, the statutory period is six weeks. The court may extend time where there is good reason to do so, but a four-month delay to explore informal resolution may or may not be accepted depending on the circumstances. The overriding requirement is promptness even within the three-month window. Options A, C and D are wrong.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional & Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: Marcus Bridgewater wins his judicial review of a planning refusal. The court wishes to quash the unlawful decision. What is the modern name for the remedy formerly called certiorari?

options.A: Mandatory order.
options.B: Quashing order — formerly certiorari; it sets aside (quashes) the unlawful decision.
options.C: Prohibiting order.
options.D: Declaration.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: The Civil Procedure (Amendment No.4) Rules 2000 renamed the prerogative remedies: (i) certiorari → quashing order (quashes an unlawful decision); (ii) mandamus → mandatory order (compels a public body to perform a public duty); (iii) prohibition → prohibiting order (prevents a public body from acting unlawfully in the future). A declaration (D) is an equitable remedy stating the legal position but not itself quashing the decision. The court grants remedies at its discretion even after a successful judicial review.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional & Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: The Wharton Magna Council passes a byelaw prohibiting any public protest within 200 metres of the Town Hall. Marcus Bridgewater challenges this as a breach of Article 10 ECHR. The Council argues public order justifies the restriction. Which analysis framework applies?

options.A: Article 10 is absolute — any restriction is automatically unlawful.
options.B: Article 10 is a qualified right: a restriction is permissible if it is (i) prescribed by law; (ii) pursues a legitimate aim listed in Article 10(2) (including public safety and prevention of disorder); and (iii) is necessary in a democratic society — i.e., proportionate to the legitimate aim (Handyside v UK (1976)).
options.C: The restriction is lawful provided a majority of councillors voted for it.
options.D: Article 10 only protects political speech by elected representatives.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: Article 10 ECHR protects freedom of expression but is a qualified right — restrictions can be justified under Article 10(2). The three-part test is: (i) the restriction must be prescribed by law (clear and accessible legal basis); (ii) it must pursue a legitimate aim from the exhaustive list in Article 10(2); (iii) it must be necessary in a democratic society — pressing social need, proportionate, and with reasons that are relevant and sufficient (Handyside v UK). A blanket 200m exclusion zone may fail the proportionality test. Options A and D misstate the nature and scope of Article 10. Option C is a pure democratic legitimacy argument that does not satisfy the Article 10(2) analysis.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional & Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: The government compulsorily acquires Ashworth Hall without paying compensation. Colonel Ashworth challenges this under the ECHR. Which provision protects his property rights?

options.A: Article 8 — right to respect for home.
options.B: Article 1 of Protocol 1 — every natural or legal person is entitled to the peaceful enjoyment of possessions; deprivation of property is only lawful in the public interest, subject to conditions provided for by law, and subject to compensation.
options.C: Article 6 — right to a fair trial.
options.D: Article 14 — prohibition of discrimination.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: Article 1 of Protocol 1 (A1P1) to the ECHR protects the peaceful enjoyment of possessions. Under A1P1, the state may deprive a person of their possessions only: (i) in the public interest; (ii) subject to conditions provided for by law; and (iii) subject to general principles of international law, which require payment of compensation. The absence of compensation in a compulsory purchase would render the acquisition unlawful under A1P1 (James v UK [1986]). Article 8 (A) may also be engaged regarding the home, but A1P1 is the primary property protection. Articles 6 and 14 are engaged in different contexts.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional & Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: Victoria Ashworth's law tutor argues that the European Communities Act 1972 was a 'constitutional statute' that could not be implicitly repealed by later inconsistent legislation. What is the legal basis for this argument?

options.A: The Diceyan principle of parliamentary sovereignty — later Acts always override earlier Acts.
options.B: The principle established in Thoburn v Sunderland CC [2002] that certain Acts of constitutional importance (including the ECA 1972, MCA 1997, HRA 1998) are 'constitutional statutes' that can only be expressly repealed, not implicitly repealed by inconsistent later legislation.
options.C: EU law requires member states to entrench EU-implementing legislation.
options.D: The common law prohibits the repeal of any legislation more than 25 years old.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: In Thoburn v Sunderland CC [2002] Laws LJ argued for a category of 'constitutional statutes' which, by virtue of their fundamental constitutional importance, cannot be implicitly repealed by later inconsistent ordinary legislation — only express repeal would suffice. He identified the ECA 1972, the MCA 1997, and the HRA 1998 as examples. This modifies the traditional Diceyan principle (A) that later legislation automatically prevails over earlier inconsistent Acts. The ECA 1972 has since been repealed expressly by the European Union (Withdrawal) Act 2018, which itself was a constitutional statute. Option C is incorrect as a basis. Option D has no legal basis.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional & Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: The government publishes a policy document promising that existing licence holders will be given 12 months' notice before any change in licensing conditions. Wharton Magna Builders Ltd holds such a licence. The government then changes the conditions with 2 weeks' notice. Which public law principle is engaged?

options.A: Parliamentary sovereignty — the government can always change policy.
options.B: Legitimate expectation — a clear and unambiguous representation by a public body to an identifiable person or group gives rise to a procedural legitimate expectation that they will be given notice or consulted before the expected benefit is removed (R v North and East Devon HA ex parte Coughlan [2001]).
options.C: Rule of law — changes to legal rules must be published.
options.D: Proportionality — only major changes require notice.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: The doctrine of legitimate expectation protects individuals who have reasonably relied on representations made by public bodies. In R v North and East Devon HA ex parte Coughlan [2001] the Court of Appeal recognised that a clear and unambiguous promise or representation by a public authority gives rise to a legitimate expectation — either procedural (the right to be heard before the benefit is withdrawn) or substantive (the right to the promised benefit itself). A published policy of 12 months' notice creates a clear expectation that licence holders will be consulted or given that notice before a change. Ignoring this expectation without good public interest justification is unlawful. Option A overstates the scope of parliamentary/executive power to override legitimate expectations.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional & Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: A statute provides that the Minister's decision 'shall not be questioned in any court'. Marcus Bridgewater wishes to challenge the decision by judicial review. Can he do so?

options.A: No — Parliament's express instruction that decisions cannot be reviewed must be followed.
options.B: Yes — courts have historically construed ouster clauses narrowly and refused to allow them to exclude judicial review of jurisdictional errors; the courts protect the rule of law by ensuring the executive remains subject to legal scrutiny (Anisminic Ltd v Foreign Compensation Commission [1969]).
options.C: No — ouster clauses are always effective.
options.D: Yes — but only if the minister acted with bad faith.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: In Anisminic Ltd v Foreign Compensation Commission [1969] the House of Lords held that a statute providing that decisions 'shall not be questioned in any court' did not protect a decision that was made in error of law — because a decision tainted by an error of law was not a 'determination' within the meaning of the statute at all. Courts have consistently read ouster clauses narrowly to preserve judicial review jurisdiction, on the basis that Parliament cannot have intended to oust review of unlawful conduct. The Supreme Court has more recently examined the tension between parliamentary sovereignty and the rule of law in Privacy International [2019]. Option A gives ouster clauses more effect than courts allow. Option C is wrong.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional & Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: Frank Hollis is detained by police under terrorism legislation for 48 hours without being brought before a court. Which Article 5 guarantee is directly engaged?

options.A: Article 5(1) — the right to liberty; detention must be within one of the exhaustive permitted grounds in Article 5(1)(a)-(f).
options.B: Article 5(3) — a person arrested on reasonable suspicion must be brought promptly before a judge; prolonged pre-charge detention without judicial oversight may violate this guarantee.
options.C: Article 5(4) — right to speedily challenge the lawfulness of detention before a court.
options.D: All of the above — all three sub-articles are simultaneously engaged by 48-hour detention without judicial oversight.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: Article 5 ECHR contains multiple inter-related guarantees: (1) Article 5(1) protects the fundamental right to liberty — any deprivation must fall within one of the exhaustive permitted categories; (2) Article 5(3) requires that a person arrested under 5(1)(c) (for criminal offences) be brought promptly before a judge; (3) Article 5(4) gives any detained person the right to challenge the lawfulness of detention speedily before a court (habeas corpus equivalent). Forty-eight hours without any judicial oversight potentially engages all three. Option D is the most complete answer.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional & Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: A national environmental NGO wishes to challenge a government decision to grant planning permission for a new coal mine in Wharton Magna. The NGO has no property interest near the mine but campaigns on environmental issues. Does it have standing to bring judicial review?

options.A: No — only persons with a direct property interest have standing in planning cases.
options.B: Yes — under CPR r.54.4, the claimant must have a 'sufficient interest' in the matter to which the application relates (Senior Courts Act 1981 s.31(3)); courts have recognised that pressure groups and NGOs with relevant expertise and concern for the subject matter may have sufficient interest (R v Foreign Secretary ex parte World Development Movement [1995]).
options.C: No — judicial review is only available to individuals, not organisations.
options.D: Yes — any citizen can challenge any government decision by judicial review.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: Senior Courts Act 1981 s.31(3) requires the claimant to have a 'sufficient interest'. In R v Foreign Secretary ex parte World Development Movement [1995] the Divisional Court held that an NGO had sufficient interest to challenge a government decision on overseas aid, despite having no direct financial or property interest. Factors included the importance of vindicating the rule of law and the absence of anyone with a greater personal interest who would challenge the decision. Environmental NGOs with relevant expertise and a demonstrable concern for the subject matter regularly satisfy the sufficient interest test in planning and environmental cases. Option A is too restrictive. Option C is wrong. Option D overstates standing.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional & Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: The Prime Minister exercises prerogative power to deploy armed forces overseas. Marcus Bridgewater seeks judicial review of this decision. What is the court likely to decide?

options.A: The court will quash the decision as unlawful.
options.B: The deployment of armed forces is a non-justiciable prerogative power; courts will decline to review decisions in this area on the basis that it involves questions of national security and foreign policy that are not susceptible to judicial review (R v Secretary of State for Foreign Affairs ex parte Everett [1989]; CCSU v Minister for the Civil Service [1985]).
options.C: The court will review the decision but apply a lower standard of scrutiny.
options.D: The Prime Minister's prerogative powers are immune from any legal challenge whatsoever.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: In CCSU v Minister for the Civil Service [1985] the House of Lords confirmed that while prerogative powers are in principle reviewable, certain prerogative decisions are non-justiciable — they are not amenable to judicial review because they involve high policy matters (national security, foreign affairs, deployment of armed forces, treaty-making) that courts are institutionally ill-equipped to assess. The deployment of armed forces falls into this category. Courts are not immune from ever reviewing any prerogative exercise (D overstates), but will decline where the subject matter is non-justiciable. Option A overstates judicial willingness to intervene. Option C understates the non-justiciability principle.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional & Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: In R (Miller) v Prime Minister [2019] (Miller No.2), the Supreme Court ruled that the Prime Minister's advice to prorogue Parliament for five weeks was unlawful. On what basis?

options.A: The prorogation breached the European Communities Act 1972.
options.B: The prorogation was unlawful because it had the effect of frustrating Parliament's ability to carry out its constitutional functions without reasonable justification — the prerogative to prorogue cannot be used in a way that defeats the constitutional principles of Parliamentary sovereignty and the accountability of the executive to Parliament.
options.C: The Queen acted unlawfully by proroguing Parliament.
options.D: Prorogation is always unlawful if it lasts more than four weeks.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: In R (Miller) v Prime Minister [2019] the Supreme Court held unanimously that the Prime Minister's advice to prorogue Parliament for five weeks (in a period of acute constitutional importance around Brexit) was unlawful. The Court reasoned that the prerogative to advise prorogation cannot be used in a way that frustrates Parliament from exercising its fundamental constitutional functions (scrutiny of the executive and legislation). There was no reasonable justification for the extended prorogation. The decision was justiciable — courts can review exercises of prerogative power for constitutional lawfulness. Option A is wrong. Option C is wrong — the Queen acted on advice. Option D invents a fixed time limit.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional & Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: The UK Parliament wishes to legislate on a matter that falls within the legislative competence of the Scottish Parliament. What constitutional convention applies?

options.A: The UK Parliament cannot legislate on devolved matters under any circumstances.
options.B: The Sewel Convention — by convention, the UK Parliament will not normally legislate on devolved matters without the consent of the devolved legislature (expressed through a legislative consent motion); this convention was given statutory recognition in the Scotland Act 2016 s.2 but remains a convention, not a legally enforceable rule.
options.C: The Scottish Parliament must hold a referendum before consenting.
options.D: The UK Parliament must obtain court approval before legislating on devolved matters.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: The Sewel Convention provides that the UK Parliament will not normally legislate with regard to devolved matters without the consent of the relevant devolved legislature. It is expressed through Legislative Consent Motions (LCMs). Scotland Act 2016 s.2 gave the convention statutory recognition ('it is recognised that the Parliament of the United Kingdom will not normally legislate with regard to devolved matters without the consent of the Scottish Parliament'), but the Supreme Court in R (Miller) v Secretary of State [2017] (Miller No.1) confirmed that it remains a political convention, not a legally enforceable obligation — the UK Parliament retains sovereignty to legislate even without consent. Option A overstates the constraint on the UK Parliament. Options C and D are wrong.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional & Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: The government introduces a restriction on the right to assembly that interferes with Article 11 ECHR. In assessing the lawfulness of the restriction under the HRA 1998, what test does the court apply?

options.A: Wednesbury unreasonableness only.
options.B: A structured proportionality analysis: (i) is the aim legitimate? (ii) is the restriction rationally connected to the aim? (iii) could a less restrictive measure achieve the aim? (iv) does the restriction strike a fair balance between the rights of the individual and the interests of the community? (De Freitas v Permanent Secretary [1999]; Bank Mellat v Her Majesty's Treasury [2013]).
options.C: The court defers entirely to the government's judgment on national security grounds.
options.D: The restriction is automatically unlawful if it infringes an Article 11 right.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: In HRA 1998 cases involving qualified Convention rights, English courts apply a structured proportionality analysis. In Bank Mellat v Her Majesty's Treasury (No.2) [2013] the Supreme Court set out a four-stage test: (i) whether the objective is sufficiently important to justify limiting the right; (ii) whether the measure is rationally connected to the objective; (iii) whether a less intrusive measure could achieve the same objective; and (iv) whether, balancing the severity of the rights restriction against the importance of the objective, a fair balance has been struck. This is more intensive than Wednesbury (A). Blanket deference (C) is inappropriate for human rights interferences. Automatic invalidity (D) is wrong — Article 11 is a qualified right with permitted restrictions.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional & Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: Marcus Bridgewater wishes to challenge a planning inspector's decision by judicial review. An alternative statutory appeal exists under the Town and Country Planning Act 1990 s.288 to the High Court. What is the court likely to do with his judicial review application?

options.A: Hear the judicial review regardless of the alternative remedy.
options.B: Likely refuse permission for judicial review on the basis that the claimant has an adequate alternative remedy available — courts generally require claimants to exhaust available alternative remedies before granting judicial review.
options.C: Grant judicial review automatically because planning decisions affect property rights.
options.D: Transfer the judicial review application to the planning inspector.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: A fundamental principle governing the grant of permission for judicial review is that the claimant must have no adequate alternative remedy (or must have exhausted available alternatives). Where Parliament has provided a specific statutory right of appeal (such as s.288 TCPA 1990), courts will ordinarily refuse judicial review and require the claimant to use the statutory route. This prevents judicial review from becoming a general appeal mechanism. Exceptions exist (e.g., the statutory route is inadequate or the point is one of pure law) but they are narrow. Options A, C and D ignore the alternative remedy principle.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional & Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: The Wharton Magna Council provides a benefit to married couples but not to unmarried couples in otherwise identical situations. A cohabiting couple challenges this under Article 14 ECHR. What must they show?

options.A: Article 14 is a free-standing right to equality that applies in all circumstances.
options.B: Article 14 prohibits discrimination in the enjoyment of the rights and freedoms set out in the Convention; the couple must show: (i) they fall within the ambit of another Convention right (e.g., Article 8 — family life); (ii) they have been treated differently from others in an analogous situation; (iii) there is no objective justification for the difference in treatment.
options.C: The couple must show the council intended to discriminate against them.
options.D: Article 14 can only be invoked by persons in protected classes defined by UK statute.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: Article 14 ECHR prohibits discrimination in the enjoyment of Convention rights and freedoms — it is not a free-standing right to equality (A). To invoke Article 14, the claimant must bring the complaint within the ambit of another Convention right. Once within the ambit, the court considers whether: (i) the claimant is treated differently from others in an analogous situation; (ii) there is an objective and reasonable justification for the different treatment (proportionality). Marital status is a ground analogous to those listed in Article 14. Unlike UK equality law, Article 14 does not require proof of intent to discriminate (C). The grounds listed in Article 14 are not exhaustive — 'other status' can include marital/relationship status (D is wrong).

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional & Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: A Minister purports to use a wide Henry VIII clause in a statute to repeal primary legislation without Parliamentary approval. Lord Judge (former Lord Chief Justice) criticises this. On what constitutional principle is this criticism based?

options.A: The rule of law and Parliamentary sovereignty — Henry VIII clauses allow the executive to amend or repeal primary legislation by secondary legislation, bypassing the normal legislative process; this concentrates power in the executive and undermines parliamentary scrutiny and the constitutional principle that primary legislation should only be changed by Parliament.
options.B: Separation of church and state.
options.C: The royal prerogative.
