options.D: EU law supremacy.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: Henry VIII clauses (so named after the Statute of Proclamations 1539, which gave Henry VIII power to legislate by proclamation) are provisions in primary legislation that grant ministers power to amend or repeal Acts of Parliament by statutory instrument. Critics argue they breach the constitutional principles of parliamentary sovereignty (primary law should be made by Parliament) and the rule of law (citizens should be governed by democratically enacted rules subject to proper scrutiny). The Delegated Powers and Regulatory Reform Committee and senior judges including Lord Judge have expressed serious concerns. Options A, C and D are wrong.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional & Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: Councillor Stubbs's planning committee refuses Marcus Bridgewater's application but gives no written reasons. Is there a general duty to give reasons for administrative decisions?

options.A: No — there is no general common law duty to give reasons, but a duty may arise in specific contexts: where statute requires it; where the decision affects a fundamental right; where there is a legitimate expectation of reasons; or where fairness demands it (R v Higher Education Funding Council ex parte Institute of Dental Surgery [1994]).
options.B: Yes — there is always a general common law duty to give reasons.
options.C: No — reasons are never required for planning decisions.
options.D: Yes — all public decisions must be accompanied by written reasons under the HRA 1998.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: English common law does not impose a general duty on all public bodies to give reasons for all decisions. However, the courts have recognised that in certain circumstances a duty to give reasons does arise: where statute requires it (e.g., the Town and Country Planning (Development Management Procedure) Order 2012 requires reasons for planning refusals, making this a statutory duty in planning); where the decision affects a person's livelihood or fundamental rights; where there is a legitimate expectation of reasons; and in some cases of important administrative decisions (Stefan v GMC [1999]). Option B overstates the common law position. Option C is wrong — planning refusals specifically require reasons by statute. Option D is wrong — HRA 1998 does not create a blanket reasons obligation.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional & Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: A prisoner dies in Wharton Magna prison in suspicious circumstances. The state's investigation is conducted internally without independent scrutiny. Which Article 2 obligation is engaged beyond the negative duty not to kill?

options.A: Article 2 imposes a positive procedural obligation to conduct an effective official investigation into deaths where the state may be implicated; the investigation must be independent, adequate, and capable of leading to the identification and punishment of those responsible (Jordan v UK (2001)).
options.B: Article 2 has no positive obligations — only the negative duty not to kill applies.
options.C: Article 3 applies instead of Article 2 where the death occurred in custody.
options.D: The state's internal investigation is always sufficient to discharge Article 2 obligations.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: Article 2 ECHR imposes both substantive and procedural obligations. The substantive obligation includes the negative duty not to take life unlawfully and positive duties to take preventive operational measures where there is a real and immediate risk to life (Osman v UK [1998]). The procedural obligation requires an effective official investigation into deaths where agents of the state are involved or where the state may bear responsibility — the investigation must be independent, adequate, reasonably prompt, and open to public scrutiny (Jordan v UK [2001]). An internal investigation without independence would breach this procedural obligation. Option B is wrong. Option C is wrong — Article 2 applies to deaths, Article 3 to treatment. Option D is wrong.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional & Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: A senior civil servant at the Department for Transport makes a catastrophic error in a major procurement process, costing taxpayers hundreds of millions of pounds. The Minister was unaware. Which constitutional convention is most directly engaged?

options.A: Individual ministerial responsibility — by convention, ministers are accountable to Parliament for the actions of their departments, including errors by civil servants acting in their name; the extent of personal responsibility depends on whether the minister knew of or directed the error.
options.B: Parliamentary sovereignty — Parliament can investigate the matter.
options.C: The Salisbury Convention — the House of Lords should not block government business.
options.D: Collective Cabinet responsibility — all ministers must collectively support the decision.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: The convention of individual ministerial responsibility requires ministers to be accountable to Parliament for the actions of their departments. However, the extent of personal accountability has evolved: following the Crichel Down Affair (1954), ministers would resign for serious errors even without personal involvement; modern practice is more nuanced — ministers are responsible for explaining errors and providing remedies, but personal resignation may only be expected where the minister was personally involved or where confidence in their leadership has been destroyed. The error by a civil servant engages this convention most directly. The Salisbury Convention (C) concerns Lords-Commons relations. Collective responsibility (D) concerns Cabinet unity.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional & Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: A judicial review claim by Marcus Bridgewater challenges a planning permission granted to Ashworth Developments Ltd. The developer has not been named as a defendant. What procedural step should be taken?

options.A: Ashworth Developments Ltd should be named as an interested party under CPR r.54.6; interested parties are directly affected by the outcome of the claim and are entitled to file an acknowledgment of service and to participate in the hearing.
options.B: The developer has no right to participate in proceedings affecting its permission.
options.C: The developer must issue a separate action to protect its interests.
options.D: Only government departments can be defendants in judicial review.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: CPR r.54.6 requires the claim form in judicial review proceedings to identify any person the claimant considers to be an interested party (a person directly affected by the claim). The permission holder (here Ashworth Developments Ltd) is directly affected by any order quashing the planning permission and must be named. Interested parties may file an acknowledgment of service and participate in the hearing. Option B is wrong — the developer has significant rights at stake and a right to participate. Option C is wrong — there is no need for separate proceedings. Option D is wrong — defendants are public bodies but interested parties can be private persons or companies.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional & Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: Can a private individual sue another private individual directly under the HRA 1998 for breach of a Convention right?

options.A: No — the HRA 1998 s.6 makes it unlawful for a public authority to act in breach of Convention rights; private individuals cannot directly sue each other under the HRA 1998, though Convention rights may have indirect horizontal effect through courts developing the common law compatibly with Convention rights.
options.B: Yes — the HRA 1998 creates direct rights between all persons.
options.C: Yes — but only for Article 8 (privacy) claims.
options.D: No — only the ECtHR can enforce Convention rights against private parties.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: HRA 1998 s.6 creates a duty only on 'public authorities' not to act incompatibly with Convention rights. A private person cannot sue another private person directly under the HRA 1998 (no 'direct horizontal effect'). However, courts are themselves public authorities and must act compatibly with Convention rights (s.6(3)(a)); this means courts must develop the common law and interpret existing law in a Convention-compatible way even in private litigation (indirect horizontal effect) — as seen in privacy and defamation cases (Campbell v MGN [2004]). Option B overstates the effect. Option C is wrong. Option D is wrong — the ECtHR only has jurisdiction against state parties, not private individuals.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional & Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.subtopic: Injunctive relief — interim relief against the Crown
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: In judicial review proceedings against a government minister, a claimant urgently seeks an interim injunction restraining the minister from implementing a decision pending the outcome of the case. In light of M v Home Office [1993], is an interim injunction available against the Crown?

options.A: Yes — following M v Home Office [1993] HL, interim injunctions are available against government ministers acting in their official capacity; the Crown is not immune from injunctive relief in public law proceedings.
options.B: No — the Crown has absolute immunity from injunctions.
options.C: Yes — but only for declarations, not injunctions.
options.D: No — only the European Court can grant injunctions against the Crown.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: In M v Home Office [1993] the House of Lords held that courts have jurisdiction to grant injunctions against ministers acting in their official capacity (as distinct from the Crown itself, to which the Crown Proceedings Act 1947 s.21 still limits injunctions). The case also confirmed that ministers can be found in contempt of court for breaching injunctions — maintaining judicial oversight of executive action. Option B incorrectly asserts absolute immunity. Option C is wrong — injunctions, not just declarations, are available. Option D is wrong.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional & Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: A local council adopts a rigid policy that all planning applications in a conservation area will automatically be refused, with no exceptions permitted. Marcus Bridgewater applies and is automatically refused. Is the policy lawful?

options.A: No — a public authority that has a statutory discretion must genuinely exercise it in each case; adopting a rigid policy that fetters the discretion by allowing no exceptions is unlawful (British Oxygen Co v Board of Trade [1971]).
options.B: Yes — a council may set firm policies to ensure consistency.
options.C: Yes — provided the policy was adopted by a full council vote.
options.D: No — only central government can create planning policies.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: In British Oxygen Co v Board of Trade [1971] the House of Lords held that a public authority may formulate a policy for the exercise of its statutory discretion, but must not so slavishly follow that policy as to refuse to listen to applicants with exceptional cases. A body with a discretion must genuinely consider each case on its merits — a rigid 'no exceptions' policy fetters the discretion and makes the refusal unlawful without proper consideration. The council may have a general presumption against development in conservation areas, but must consider individual applications. Options A and C permit an unlawful rigidity. Option D is wrong.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional & Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: Following the repeal of the Fixed-term Parliaments Act 2011 by the Dissolution and Calling of Parliament Act 2022, how is Parliament now dissolved?

options.A: Parliament is dissolved by prerogative power — the monarch, on the advice of the Prime Minister, dissolves Parliament; the FTPA 2011's fixed-term and early-election mechanisms have been repealed and the prerogative restored.
options.B: Parliament dissolves automatically at the end of a five-year fixed term.
options.C: Parliament can only be dissolved by a two-thirds vote of the House of Commons.
options.D: Parliament can only be dissolved by Act of Parliament.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: The Fixed-term Parliaments Act 2011 introduced fixed five-year terms and a mechanism for early elections requiring a two-thirds majority. The Dissolution and Calling of Parliament Act 2022 repealed the FTPA 2011 and restored the monarch's prerogative power to dissolve Parliament on the advice of the Prime Minister. The Act also expressly provides that the decision to dissolve and the calling of a new Parliament are non-justiciable. Options A and C describe the now-repealed FTPA regime. Option D is wrong — no Act of Parliament is needed.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional & Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.subtopic: Costs in judicial review — protective costs orders
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: An environmental NGO brings a judicial review challenging a government environmental decision. The NGO is concerned about costs exposure if it loses. What costs protection mechanism may be available?

options.A: A Protective Costs Order (PCO) / Cost Capping Order under CPR r.46.16-17 — in public interest litigation, a court may cap the claimant's liability for the defendant's costs if the case raises an issue of genuine public importance and it would not be reasonable to expect the claimant to proceed without costs protection (Corner House Research [2005]).
options.B: No costs protection is available in judicial review.
options.C: Legal aid is automatically available to NGOs bringing environmental claims.
options.D: The claimant always pays the defendant's costs in judicial review.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: In R (Corner House Research) v Secretary of State for Trade and Industry [2005] the Court of Appeal set out the criteria for Protective Costs Orders (now Cost Capping Orders under CPR r.46.16-17) in public interest litigation: (i) the case raises issues of genuine public importance; (ii) the public interest requires the case to be resolved; (iii) the claimant has no private interest in the outcome; (iv) the claimant would not proceed without costs protection; (v) it is fair and just in all the circumstances. In Aarhus Convention cases (environmental judicial review), CPR r.45.41-44 provides specific fixed costs protection for qualifying claimants. Option B is wrong. Option C is wrong. Option D is wrong — the normal rule of costs following the event applies, subject to PCO/CCO.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional & Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: A court finds that a public authority has violated Marcus Bridgewater's Convention rights. In deciding whether to award damages under HRA 1998 s.8, what principle does the court apply?

options.A: The court may award damages only if it is necessary to afford just satisfaction under s.8(3), having regard to the principles applied by the ECtHR; damages are not automatically awarded — a declaration or other relief may be sufficient.
options.B: Damages are automatically awarded for every Convention breach.
options.C: HRA damages are assessed on the same basis as common law tort damages.
options.D: Only the ECtHR can award damages for Convention breaches.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: HRA 1998 s.8 provides that where a court finds a public authority has acted unlawfully (in breach of s.6), it may grant such relief or remedy as it considers just and appropriate, including damages. However, s.8(3) restricts the power to award damages to cases where it is necessary to afford just satisfaction. The court is guided by the principles of the ECtHR, which often awards modest 'just satisfaction' rather than full compensatory damages. A declaration that rights have been violated may be sufficient in some cases. HRA damages are not assessed the same as common law tort damages (C). Option D is wrong — domestic courts have this power under s.8.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional & Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.subtopic: Advising on judicial review — prospects assessment
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: Gerald Crane is instructed to bring a judicial review on behalf of Marcus Bridgewater. Crane privately thinks the claim has very limited prospects of obtaining permission. He does not share this view with Bridgewater. Which SRA Code obligation is most directly engaged?

options.A: The duty to act in clients' best interests includes providing an honest assessment of the merits and prospects of litigation, even if this advice is unwelcome; withholding this assessment breaches para 1.2 and para 1.4 (SRA Code).
options.B: No obligation — the client gives instructions and the solicitor follows them.
options.C: The duty of confidentiality prevents Crane from sharing his views.
options.D: The duty to the court requires Crane to disclose his doubts to the Administrative Court.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: SRA Principle 7 requires solicitors to act in the best interests of each client, which includes giving honest advice about the merits and risks of litigation — even when this is unwelcome. Para 1.4 requires solicitors not to mislead or attempt to mislead their clients. Withholding an honest assessment of poor prospects deprives the client of information essential for an informed decision about whether to pursue costly proceedings. SRA Code para 3.4 also requires solicitors not to suppress their professional judgment. The duty of confidentiality (C) concerns disclosure to third parties, not honesty with one's own client. Disclosure to the court (D) is not required — though a solicitor should not make representations about merits they do not believe.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional and Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: Marcus Bridgewater challenges a Home Office decision refusing him indefinite leave to remain on grounds it interferes with his Article 8 ECHR rights. The Upper Tribunal applies proportionality review. Which statement best describes the proportionality analysis under Bank Mellat v HM Treasury (No 2) [2013]?

options.A: The court applies a structured four-stage test: legitimate aim, rational connection, no less restrictive alternative, and fair balance between individual and community interests.
options.B: The court asks only whether the decision is manifestly disproportionate, deferring entirely to ministerial judgment on necessity.
options.C: Proportionality under the HRA is identical to Wednesbury unreasonableness and adds no additional scrutiny.
options.D: The court may only apply proportionality when the claimant relies on an absolute Convention right such as Article 3.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: Bank Mellat (No 2) [2013] UKSC 39 confirmed a structured four-stage proportionality analysis: (i) sufficiently important objective; (ii) rational connection between measure and objective; (iii) no less restrictive alternative; (iv) fair balance. This is more intensive than Wednesbury and applies across qualified rights. Option B conflates proportionality with an older margin of appreciation approach; C incorrectly equates the tests; D wrongly limits proportionality to absolute rights.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional and Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.subtopic: Article 9 ECHR — freedom of thought, conscience and religion
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: Donna Pearce, a care home employee, is dismissed after refusing to work Sunday shifts on religious grounds. She argues her Article 9 ECHR rights were violated. The employer is a private company. Which statement correctly identifies the legal position?

options.A: Article 9 ECHR binds private employers directly under the HRA 1998, so the dismissal is automatically unlawful.
options.B: Article 9 is an absolute right and cannot be interfered with by any employer, public or private.
options.C: The HRA does not bind private employers directly, but courts as public authorities must develop common law compatibly with Convention rights, and employment tribunals must interpret the Equality Act 2010 compatibly with Article 9.
options.D: Article 9 protection is limited to attendance at religious services and does not extend to refusal to work on a particular day.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: The HRA 1998 s.6 binds public authorities, not private employers directly. However, tribunals as public authorities (s.6) must act compatibly with Convention rights, and s.3 requires legislation (including EA 2010) to be read compatibly. Article 9(2) allows interference for legitimate aims proportionately pursued — it is a qualified, not absolute, right. Courts develop the common law consistently with Convention rights (Campbell v MGN). Options A and C overstate horizontal direct effect; D wrongly narrows the scope of Article 9.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional and Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.subtopic: Article 11 ECHR — freedom of assembly and association
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: Councillor Stubbs issues a dispersal order under the Police, Crime, Sentencing and Courts Act 2022 preventing a protest group organised by Rachel Okonkwo from assembling in the town square. Rachel seeks judicial review arguing a breach of Article 11. Which ground is most likely to succeed?

options.A: The order is unlawful because Article 11 confers an absolute right to peaceful assembly that cannot be restricted.
options.B: Dispersal orders under statute can never be challenged in judicial review as Parliament has authorised them.
options.C: The order may be unlawful if it was not prescribed by law, did not pursue a legitimate aim, or was not necessary in a democratic society — the court applying proportionality review.
options.D: Article 11 does not apply to outdoor protests; its protection is limited to indoor meetings.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: Article 11(2) permits interference with assembly and association rights where it is: (i) prescribed by law; (ii) pursues a legitimate aim (public safety, prevention of disorder, etc.); (iii) necessary in a democratic society (proportionate). Courts apply structured proportionality review under the HRA. The power must be exercised compatibly; a blanket ban without particularised justification may be disproportionate. A is wrong — Article 11 is qualified; C is wrong — statutory powers must be exercised compatibly with HRA; D has no basis in law.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional and Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.subtopic: Judicial deference and the separation of powers
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: In a judicial review of a government decision to deploy troops abroad, the court is asked to assess whether the operation is consistent with international law. Which principle best explains why the court is likely to decline to adjudicate the substantive military question?

options.A: The court lacks jurisdiction over any matter involving the executive branch.
options.B: The principle of parliamentary sovereignty means courts must follow executive decisions on military matters.
options.C: Decisions involving high policy, foreign affairs and national security are non-justiciable or attract a wide margin of appreciation, and courts will not second-guess complex military and political judgments.
options.D: Judicial review is only available for domestic decisions; international matters are entirely excluded from the supervisory jurisdiction.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: Courts recognise areas of non-justiciability — matters so inherently political or requiring specialist expertise (military strategy, foreign policy) that judicial intervention is inappropriate (Council of Civil Service Unions v Minister for the Civil Service [1985]). This is distinct from an absolute bar on jurisdiction; courts retain supervisory jurisdiction but apply a wide margin of appreciation in high-policy areas. A is too absolute — courts have jurisdiction but exercise restraint; C misstates parliamentary sovereignty; D is incorrect — domestic courts can consider international law issues.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional and Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: A declaration of incompatibility has been made in respect of a provision of the Immigration Act. The relevant minister wishes to amend the legislation quickly without a full parliamentary bill. Which mechanism enables this?

options.A: The minister may unilaterally repeal the incompatible provision by executive order without parliamentary involvement.
options.B: No amendment is possible without a full Act of Parliament — delegated legislation cannot amend primary legislation.
options.C: The minister may use a remedial order under s.10 HRA 1998 to amend primary legislation by statutory instrument, subject to parliamentary scrutiny under Schedule 2.
options.D: The minister must apply to the Supreme Court for an order directing Parliament to legislate.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: Section 10 HRA 1998 provides a 'fast-track' procedure: where a declaration of incompatibility has been made (or a judgment of the ECtHR requires it), a minister may make a remedial order by statutory instrument to amend the incompatible legislation. Schedule 2 HRA sets out parliamentary scrutiny requirements (usually super-affirmative procedure). This is a Henry VIII power specifically created by the HRA. A is wrong — parliamentary involvement is required; C is incorrect — s.10 expressly permits SI amendment of primary legislation; D does not reflect the legal position.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional and Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: The Senedd passes legislation regulating land transactions in Wales. Westminster subsequently passes an Act of Parliament covering the same subject matter without consulting Cardiff. Which statement best describes the constitutional position?

options.A: The Westminster Act is invalid because devolution permanently transferred land law competence to the Senedd.
options.B: The Senedd has exclusive competence over land law in Wales and Westminster legislation automatically ceases to apply.
options.C: Westminster retains parliamentary sovereignty and may legislate for Wales on any matter, but the Sewel Convention requires it to seek the Senedd's legislative consent for devolved matters.
options.D: Devolved legislation always takes precedence over Westminster Acts in Wales by virtue of the Wales Act 2017.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: Parliamentary sovereignty means Westminster retains legislative competence over all matters including Wales (Wales Act 2017 s.2 restates supremacy). The Sewel Convention (now reflected in Wales Act 2017 s.107(6)) requires Westminster to seek legislative consent from the Senedd before legislating in devolved areas. However, in Miller [2017] the Supreme Court confirmed the convention is not legally enforceable — Parliament may legislate without consent. The Senedd's competence is conferred by statute and subject to reserved matters. A and C are wrong; D misstates the hierarchy.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional and Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.subtopic: Northern Ireland — Belfast Agreement and the Assembly
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: A Northern Ireland Assembly Act purports to legislate on a 'excepted' matter under Schedule 2 to the Northern Ireland Act 1998. The Attorney General refers the question to the Supreme Court. What is the likely outcome?

options.A: The Act is valid because the Northern Ireland Assembly has unfettered legislative competence.
options.B: The Supreme Court has no jurisdiction to review Northern Ireland Assembly legislation.
options.C: The Act is outside the Assembly's legislative competence and the court will make a declaration accordingly, with the provision having no effect.
options.D: Excepted matters may be legislated on by the Assembly with the Secretary of State's written consent.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: The Northern Ireland Act 1998 s.6 provides that an Assembly Act is not law to the extent it is outside legislative competence. Schedule 2 lists 'excepted matters' (reserved exclusively to Westminster, e.g., the Crown, international relations, defence). The Supreme Court has jurisdiction to determine legislative competence under s.11 NIA 1998. A provision on an excepted matter has no effect. Option D describes the position for 'reserved matters' (Schedule 3), not excepted matters. Options A and C are incorrect.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional and Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.subtopic: FOIA 2000 — qualified exemptions and public interest test
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: Ravi Sharma makes a FOIA request to Wharton Borough Council for internal communications relating to a planning decision affecting Ashworth Developments Ltd. The council refuses under s.36 FOIA (prejudice to effective conduct of public affairs). What must the council demonstrate?

options.A: Disclosure would absolutely harm the conduct of public affairs — no balancing exercise is required.
options.B: The information is entirely exempt from disclosure because it relates to a local authority decision.
options.C: The qualified person has given a reasonable opinion that disclosure would or would be likely to inhibit free and frank discussion, and the public interest in maintaining the exemption outweighs the public interest in disclosure.
options.D: The council need only show the information is commercially sensitive to rely on s.36.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: Section 36 FOIA is a qualified exemption applicable to public authorities (other than government departments for s.36(2)(c)). The 'qualified person' (usually the Speaker/minister/monitoring officer) must give a reasonable opinion that one of the specified prejudices would occur. Being qualified, the exemption is subject to the public interest test in s.2 FOIA — the public interest in maintaining the exemption must outweigh the public interest in disclosure. A wrongly treats it as absolute; C and D misstate the basis for the exemption.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional and Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.subtopic: Natural justice — rule against bias (nemo iudex)
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: A planning inspector conducting an inquiry into an application by Ashworth Developments Ltd is discovered to hold shares in a rival development company. Diana Pelletier, objecting to the development, applies for judicial review. Which test determines whether the inspector is disqualified?

options.A: Whether the inspector actually allowed the shareholding to influence the decision (subjective bias).
options.B: Whether the inspector's interest is so substantial that it automatically disqualifies them regardless of any appearance (automatic disqualification for pecuniary interest).
options.C: Whether a fair-minded and informed observer would conclude there is a real possibility that the decision-maker was biased (the Porter v Magill objective test).
options.D: Whether the claimant can prove on the balance of probabilities that the inspector consciously preferred the rival company's interests.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: Porter v Magill [2001] UKHL 67 established that the test for apparent bias is whether a fair-minded and informed observer, having considered the facts, would conclude there is a real possibility that the tribunal was biased. A financial interest in a rival (rather than the applicant) would be analysed under this objective test rather than automatic disqualification (which applies to direct financial interests in the outcome — Dimes v Grand Junction Canal). Options A and D require proof of actual bias; B applies to direct financial interests in the proceedings, not a rival company.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional and Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: The Secretary of State for Health publishes a policy document stating that NHS patients who have been waiting more than 18 weeks will receive priority treatment. Hassan Nazari has waited 22 weeks and is told the policy no longer applies following budget cuts. He seeks judicial review. Which statement best reflects the law on substantive legitimate expectation?

options.A: Substantive legitimate expectations are not recognised in English administrative law; only procedural expectations can be enforced.
options.B: The Secretary of State may freely depart from any published policy without any obligation to Hassan because policy documents are not legally binding.
options.C: A clear and unambiguous promise or established practice may give rise to a substantive legitimate expectation; the authority may only depart from it if there is an overriding public interest justification proportionate to the impact on the individual.
options.D: Once a legitimate expectation arises, the authority is absolutely bound by the original promise and cannot depart from it under any circumstances.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: English courts recognise substantive legitimate expectations following R v North and East Devon HA ex p Coughlan [2001]. Where there is a clear, unambiguous representation to an individual or group, the court may hold the authority to its promise unless: (i) it is unlawful to do so; or (ii) there is a sufficiently overriding public interest justification, proportionately pursued. The court must balance the public interest against the impact on the individual. A is wrong — substantive expectations are recognised; C overstates the freedom to depart; D is too absolute.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional and Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: A statutory tribunal interprets its enabling Act as excluding a class of applicants from its jurisdiction. The applicant argues this interpretation is wrong in law. Following Anisminic Ltd v Foreign Compensation Commission [1969] and Cart v Upper Tribunal [2011], which proposition correctly states the supervisory jurisdiction of the High Court?

options.A: The High Court cannot review errors of law made within the tribunal's jurisdiction — only 'excess of jurisdiction' is reviewable.
options.B: Only the Supreme Court can correct errors of law made by specialist tribunals.
options.C: Judicial review of tribunal decisions on legal interpretation is barred by the principle of statutory finality.
options.D: Following Anisminic, all errors of law by inferior tribunals are jurisdictional; the High Court retains supervisory jurisdiction over all errors of law, subject to Cart limitations on upper tribunal review.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: Anisminic [1969] AC 147 collapsed the distinction between jurisdictional and non-jurisdictional errors: any error of law by an inferior body is a jurisdictional error subject to judicial review. Cart v Upper Tribunal [2011] UKSC 28 (subsequently refined by R (Cart) considerations and the Judicial Review and Courts Act 2022) addressed the Upper Tribunal specifically. The general principle remains that superior courts supervise inferior tribunals for legal error. A reflects the pre-Anisminic position; Option D is wrong; C is incorrect — finality clauses are limited by Anisminic.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional and Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: Priya Mehta obtains a declaration from the Administrative Court that the Secretary of State's policy on welfare benefits is unlawful. The Secretary of State maintains the policy in place. What is the legal effect?

options.A: The declaration is automatically enforceable and the Secretary of State may be imprisoned for contempt if the policy continues.
options.B: Declarations are only advisory opinions and create no legal obligation whatsoever.
options.C: The effect of a declaration is to immediately and automatically void the impugned policy without further order.
options.D: A declaration is a discretionary remedy that pronounces the legal position but is not directly coercive; in practice, public authorities comply, but non-compliance must be enforced through further proceedings such as mandamus or contempt (if an injunction is also granted).

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: A declaration is a discretionary remedy that authoritatively states the law but does not directly compel action (R v Secretary of State for Employment ex p Equal Opportunities Commission [1995]). Public authorities conventionally comply. Enforcement of compliance requires additional orders — a mandatory order (mandamus) or an injunction — and contempt proceedings may follow breach of a court order, not the declaration itself. M v Home Office [1994] confirmed even the Crown can be found in contempt for breach of an injunction. Option B understates the legal significance; C is incorrect; A overstates the direct enforceability of a bare declaration.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional and Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: A statutory power to grant emergency licences is vested in the Secretary of State for Business. A senior civil servant in the department exercises the power without the Secretary of State's specific awareness of this particular decision. Is this lawful?

options.A: No — all statutory powers vested in a minister must be personally exercised by that minister and cannot be delegated.
options.B: Yes, but only if the civil servant holds a grade above Deputy Secretary.
options.C: Yes, but only if Parliament has expressly authorised delegation in the enabling Act.
options.D: Yes — under the Carltona principle, civil servants exercising statutory powers vested in a minister act as the minister's alter ego; the minister remains constitutionally responsible.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: The Carltona principle (Carltona Ltd v Commissioners of Works [1943]) holds that powers conferred on a minister may be exercised by officials of appropriate seniority within the department without express statutory authorisation to delegate. The minister remains constitutionally and politically responsible for the exercise of the power. The principle reflects the constitutional reality of modern government. Express statutory authorisation is not required (distinguishing Carltona delegation from delegation to outside bodies). A is wrong; C and D add requirements not found in the Carltona principle.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional and Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.subtopic: Crown immunity and public interest immunity
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: In judicial review proceedings brought by Gerald Crane, the government claims public interest immunity (PII) over certain documents on the ground of national security. The court must decide whether to uphold the claim. Which statement best describes the court's role?

options.A: The court must accept any ministerial certificate claiming PII without further inquiry.
options.B: PII is an absolute right of the executive that cannot be challenged in judicial proceedings.
options.C: PII claims automatically prevail over disclosure obligations in all cases involving national security.
options.D: The court must balance the public interest in non-disclosure against the public interest in the administration of justice, and may inspect the documents privately (in camera) to make that assessment.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: Following Conway v Rimmer [1968] AC 910 and R v Chief Constable of West Midlands Police ex p Wiley [1995], courts do not simply accept ministerial PII certificates. They conduct a balancing exercise: the public interest in non-disclosure (e.g. national security, candour) weighed against the public interest in the administration of justice. The court may inspect documents privately. The Conway v Rimmer decision overruled the House of Lords' earlier deference in Conway v Rimmer and confirmed judicial oversight. Options A, C and D reflect the pre-1968 position, which is no longer law.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional and Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: Alek Kowalski, a local resident living 3 km from a proposed industrial facility, applies for judicial review of the planning permission granted to Hollowell Engineering by Wharton Borough Council. Has he sufficient standing?

options.A: Only immediately adjacent neighbours have standing to challenge planning decisions in judicial review.
options.B: Alek must demonstrate he is directly employed in the local area to satisfy the sufficient interest test.
options.C: Standing in planning judicial review is governed by the Town and Country Planning Act 1990 and requires Alek to have made representations at the planning inquiry.
options.D: Under s.31(3) Senior Courts Act 1981, the court grants permission if the claimant has sufficient interest in the matter; proximity, local connection and potential impact on amenity may collectively satisfy this, even at 3 km.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: Section 31(3) Senior Courts Act 1981 requires 'sufficient interest'. Courts have interpreted this broadly (R v IRC ex p National Federation of Self-Employed [1982]). In planning cases, local residents who may be affected by environmental or amenity impacts typically have sufficient interest even without living immediately adjacent. Prior participation in the planning process is relevant but not always required. A and B apply unduly narrow criteria; D incorrectly substitutes a statutory test from TCPA for the JR standing test.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional and Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.subtopic: Challenge to delegated legislation — ultra vires
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: The Minister for Housing makes regulations under the Housing Act 2020 s.45, which provides power to make regulations 'for the purposes of this Act'. The regulations introduce a new criminal offence not contemplated by the 2020 Act. Rachel Okonkwo is prosecuted under the regulation. Which ground of challenge is strongest?

options.A: The regulation is ultra vires because it goes beyond the scope of the enabling power — creating criminal offences requires express Parliamentary authority.
options.B: The regulation is valid because a minister always has implied power to create criminal offences when exercising delegated legislation powers.
options.C: The challenge must be brought within three months of the regulation being made, otherwise Rachel cannot rely on its invalidity at her prosecution.
options.D: Criminal courts cannot consider the vires of delegated legislation; only the Administrative Court can do so.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: Delegated legislation must remain within the scope of the enabling power (the ultra vires doctrine). Courts will not readily imply power to create criminal offences — clear and express statutory language is required for penal provisions (R v Secretary of State for the Home Department ex p Fire Brigades Union [1995]; principle of legality). A general purpose clause does not suffice. B incorrectly implies inherent ministerial power; C is wrong — invalidity of delegated legislation may be raised as a defence in criminal proceedings without a prior JR (Boddington v British Transport Police [1999]); D is incorrect for the same reason.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional and Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.subtopic: EU law general principles — retained law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: Following the Retained EU Law (Revocation and Reform) Act 2023, a claimant argues that retained EU general principles of law (such as the principle of effectiveness) still apply to challenge a domestic decision made after the end of the transition period. What is the correct legal position?

options.A: Retained EU general principles of law continue to apply in full and take precedence over domestic law under the European Communities Act 1972.
options.B: EU general principles apply only in cases where the claimant is an EU national.
options.C: EU general principles such as proportionality and effectiveness are now part of the common law and apply with full EU supremacy.
options.D: The Retained EU Law Act 2023 abolished the supremacy of retained EU law (from 1 January 2024) and the general principles of EU law no longer form part of domestic law so as to give rise to free-standing rights of action.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: The Retained EU Law (Revocation and Reform) Act 2023 ended the supremacy of retained EU law from 1 January 2024 (s.3) and provided that retained EU general principles no longer give rise to rights of action or grounds of challenge after that date (s.4–5). Some EU-derived rights remain via separate statutory provisions, but free-standing reliance on EU general principles as such is no longer available. Option A refers to the pre-REUL Act position; C mischaracterises the current status; D has no legal basis.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional and Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: The Joint Committee on Human Rights publishes a report concluding that a Bill before Parliament is incompatible with the ECHR. The government disagrees and proceeds to pass the Bill. What is the legal effect of the Committee's conclusion?

options.A: The Bill cannot receive Royal Assent until the incompatibility identified by the Committee is resolved.
options.B: The Committee's finding creates a presumption of incompatibility that the courts must apply when interpreting the resulting Act.
options.C: The Committee's report automatically triggers a reference to the ECtHR before the Bill becomes law.
options.D: The Committee's conclusion is not legally binding; Parliament may pass the Bill, but the report informs parliamentary debate and may lead to scrutiny amendments.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: The JCHR scrutinises legislation for Convention compatibility but its conclusions are not legally binding on Parliament. Parliamentary sovereignty (confirmed by AXA General Insurance v Lord Advocate [2011]) means Parliament may legislate even if incompatibility is identified. The s.19 HRA 1998 ministerial statement of compatibility is a political/constitutional requirement but not a legal precondition for validity. Courts will later apply s.3 HRA to read legislation compatibly or make a declaration under s.4 — but the JCHR report has no direct legal effect in itself. Options A, C and D are wrong.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional and Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.subtopic: Constitutional reform — Supreme Court and judicial independence
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: Which of the following best describes the significance of the Constitutional Reform Act 2005 for judicial independence in England and Wales?

options.A: It abolished the role of Lord Chancellor and replaced it with an elected Attorney General.
options.B: It incorporated the ECHR into domestic law for the first time.
options.C: It transferred all judicial appointment powers to the Prime Minister.
options.D: It created the Supreme Court of the United Kingdom, separated the judicial functions of the House of Lords, removed the Lord Chancellor's judicial role, and established the Judicial Appointments Commission to depoliticise judicial selection.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: The Constitutional Reform Act 2005 made three principal changes: (i) created the Supreme Court of the UK (replacing the Appellate Committee of the House of Lords, operational from 2009); (ii) reformed the office of Lord Chancellor, ending the Lord Chancellor's judicial role and removing them from presiding over the Lords in a judicial capacity; (iii) established the Judicial Appointments Commission to make judicial selection more transparent and independent of political influence. Option A misstates the outcome — the Lord Chancellor was reformed, not abolished; C is wrong; D describes the HRA 1998.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional and Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: Wharton Borough Council issues an enforcement notice against Ashworth Developments Ltd requiring demolition of a structure erected without planning permission. James Ashworth argues the notice is invalid because it was served out of time under s.171B TCPA 1990. He applies for judicial review. What is the likely outcome on permission?

options.A: Permission will be refused automatically because planning disputes must be resolved by the Planning Inspectorate and cannot be judicially reviewed.
options.B: Judicial review of enforcement notices is expressly prohibited by the TCPA 1990.
options.C: The High Court will grant permission and hear the substantive challenge immediately without requiring James to exhaust appeal rights.
options.D: Permission is likely to be refused on the ground that James has an adequate alternative remedy — the right of appeal to the Planning Inspector under s.174 TCPA 1990 — making JR the inappropriate route.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: Courts exercise discretion at the permission stage and will ordinarily refuse JR where an adequate alternative remedy exists (R v IRC ex p Preston [1985]). A right of appeal to the Planning Inspector under s.174 TCPA 1990 is the normal mechanism to challenge enforcement notices, including on time-bar grounds. JR is reserved for cases where the alternative remedy is inadequate. The court would normally require James to use the statutory appeal route. A and D overstate a complete bar; C disregards the alternative remedy principle.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional and Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.subtopic: Police powers — stop and search judicial review
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: Marcus Bridgewater is stopped and searched by police using a s.60 Criminal Justice and Public Order Act 1994 power authorised by a senior officer for a designated area including the town centre. He seeks judicial review arguing the authorisation was unlawful. Which argument is most likely to succeed?

options.A: Section 60 powers cannot be subject to judicial review because they are conferred by statute.
options.B: The three-month time limit does not apply to challenges to s.60 authorisations.
options.C: Marcus must show actual racial discrimination to succeed in a judicial review of a s.60 authorisation.
options.D: The authorisation may be challenged if the authorising officer did not have reasonable grounds to believe violence may occur, or if the conditions for authorisation under s.60 were not met — the court applying public law grounds of review.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: Section 60 CJPOA 1994 authorisations are public law decisions subject to judicial review on standard grounds: illegality (conditions not met), irrationality, procedural impropriety. The statute requires the authorising officer (superintendent or above) to reasonably believe incidents involving serious violence may occur. Failure to meet this threshold renders the authorisation unlawful. A is wrong — statutory powers are judicially reviewable; C wrongly limits the grounds to discrimination; D is incorrect — the three-month time limit under CPR r.54.5 applies.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional and Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.subtopic: Public sector equality duty — s.149 Equality Act 2010
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: Wharton Borough Council proposes to close the only leisure centre in an area with a high elderly population. Priya Mehta brings judicial review arguing the council failed to have 'due regard' to the public sector equality duty under s.149 Equality Act 2010. Which statement is correct?

options.A: The PSED requires councils to achieve a specific equality outcome; failure to reduce inequality automatically renders the decision unlawful.
options.B: Compliance with the PSED is a political matter beyond judicial supervision.
options.C: The PSED only applies to decisions on employment matters, not service delivery decisions.
options.D: The PSED requires a public authority to have 'due regard' to protected characteristics before making decisions; the court reviews whether the authority consciously addressed the duty — the duty is to consider, not necessarily to achieve any particular outcome.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: Section 149 Equality Act 2010 requires public authorities to have 'due regard' to equality objectives (eliminating discrimination, advancing equality, fostering good relations) when exercising functions. Courts review whether the authority genuinely considered the PSED at the time of decision (R (Brown) v Secretary of State for Work and Pensions [2008]; Bracking v Secretary of State for Work and Pensions [2013]). The duty is procedural — to consider — not to achieve any outcome. A incorrectly treats it as outcome-based; C incorrectly restricts its scope; D wrongly insulates it from judicial review.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional and Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: The Secretary of State proposes to close a number of job centres. A public consultation is launched. However, the consultation document contains no information about the projected impact on local communities and no reasonable period for responses. Diana Pelletier challenges the decision. On which ground is she most likely to succeed?

options.A: Procedural impropriety — the consultation was so unfair as to be unlawful, failing the Gunning principles requiring adequate information, adequate time and genuine consideration of responses.
options.B: Illegality — there is no statutory power to close job centres.
options.C: Wednesbury unreasonableness — no rational minister would close job centres.
options.D: Human rights — the closure violates a positive obligation under Article 1 Protocol 1 to provide employment centres.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: Where a duty to consult exists (statutory or arising from legitimate expectation), it must meet the minimum standards of fairness identified in R v North and East Devon HA ex p Coughlan (and the Gunning criteria from R v Brent LBC ex p Gunning [1985]): (i) the proposal must be at a formative stage; (ii) sufficient reasons/information must be provided; (iii) adequate time must be given; (iv) responses must be conscientiously considered. An inadequate consultation document and insufficient response period render the process unlawful. A, B and D do not fit the scenario as well as the procedural ground.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional and Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.subtopic: Article 6 ECHR — right to a fair trial in civil proceedings
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: Gerald Crane is involved in a civil dispute with a public authority over planning compensation. The only mechanism to challenge the authority's determination is an internal review with no right to an independent external hearing. He argues a breach of Article 6(1) ECHR. Is his argument sound?

options.A: Article 6 applies only to criminal proceedings; civil property disputes are outside its scope.
options.B: Planning disputes are specifically excluded from Article 6 by Protocol 1 ECHR.
options.C: Article 6 is satisfied so long as any internal review procedure exists.
options.D: Article 6(1) applies to the determination of civil rights and obligations; access to an independent and impartial tribunal is required. An internal review without external judicial oversight may breach Article 6 unless there is access to a court with full jurisdiction on the merits.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: Article 6(1) ECHR guarantees the right to a fair hearing by an independent and impartial tribunal for the determination of civil rights and obligations (which includes property and planning rights: Sporrong and Lönnroth v Sweden). Where initial decision-making is by an authority without full independence, Article 6 compliance requires that a court with 'full jurisdiction' is available to review the merits (Bryan v UK [1995]). An internal review alone, without access to an independent external body, is likely insufficient. A is wrong; C is wrong; D has no basis in the Convention.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional and Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: Rachel Okonkwo applies for an interim injunction to prevent the demolition of a listed building by Wharton Borough Council pending her judicial review application. The council argues the balance of convenience favours demolition. Which test does the court apply?

options.A: The court grants an interim injunction only if the claimant demonstrates a certainty of success at the final hearing.
options.B: The court grants an injunction automatically on a JR permission grant to preserve the status quo.
options.C: Interim injunctions are never available against public authorities in judicial review proceedings.
options.D: The American Cyanamid test: (i) a serious issue to be tried; (ii) the balance of convenience (adequacy of damages, status quo); and in public law cases consideration of the public interest.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: The American Cyanamid Co v Ethicon [1975] test applies to interim injunctions: (i) there is a serious issue to be tried; (ii) if yes, would damages be an adequate remedy for the claimant? If not, would they be adequate for the defendant? (iii) balance of convenience. In judicial review against public authorities, additional factors include the public interest in the challenged action proceeding (or not). Injunctions against the Crown are available following M v Home Office [1994]. A sets too high a threshold; C is wrong; D is incorrect.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional and Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: The Prime Minister concludes an international trade treaty by exercise of the royal prerogative. Ravi Sharma argues Parliament must approve the treaty before it can become binding in domestic law. Which statement is correct?

options.A: Treaties concluded by the royal prerogative automatically form part of domestic law without Parliamentary approval.
options.B: Treaties require a formal Act of Parliament for conclusion but not for domestic legal effect.
options.C: The House of Lords has a veto over ratification of all international treaties.
options.D: Treaty-making is a prerogative power; however, under the Constitutional Reform and Governance Act 2010, treaties must be laid before Parliament for 21 sitting days and may be rejected by a resolution of the House of Commons before ratification.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: Treaty-making is a royal prerogative exercisable by the executive. The dualist system means treaties do not automatically form part of domestic law — an Act of Parliament is required to give them domestic legal effect (J H Rayner (Mincing Lane) Ltd v Department of Trade and Industry [1990]). The Constitutional Reform and Governance Act 2010 (CRaG) codified the Ponsonby Rule: treaties must be laid before Parliament for 21 sitting days; the House of Commons may resolve against ratification. The Lords may also resolve against, but the Commons can override. A is wrong (dualism); C overstates the Lords' role; D conflates treaty conclusion with domestic incorporation.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional and Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.subtopic: Judicial Review and Courts Act 2022 — prospective quashing orders
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: The High Court finds that a statutory instrument made in 2021 was procedurally unlawful. The claimant seeks a quashing order. The court is considering whether to make a 'suspended quashing order' under the Judicial Review and Courts Act 2022. What does this enable the court to do?

options.A: Nothing — the 2022 Act removed the court's power to quash statutory instruments.
options.B: The 2022 Act requires the court to make a suspended order in all cases where a statutory instrument is found unlawful.
options.C: A suspended quashing order permanently preserves the validity of the impugned instrument.
options.D: The court may delay the effect of the quashing order to a future date or make the order prospective only (not having retrospective effect), allowing the government time to remedy the unlawfulness and protecting intervening transactions.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: The Judicial Review and Courts Act 2022 s.1 inserted new s.29A Senior Courts Act 1981, enabling courts to make 'prospective-only' quashing orders or to suspend quashing orders to a future date. This allows courts to give effect to the quashing while limiting disruption caused by retrospective invalidity — e.g. protecting people who acted in reliance on the instrument. The court has discretion whether to use this power, weighing legal certainty, rights of affected parties and the public interest. A is wrong; C mischaracterises a suspended order; D is wrong — it is discretionary.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional and Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.subtopic: Costs in judicial review — public interest litigation
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: A public interest environmental group, the Wharton Ecology Trust, brings judicial review of a major infrastructure decision. They apply for a Protective Costs Order (PCO) to cap their adverse costs exposure before permission is granted. On what basis may a PCO be granted?

options.A: PCOs are available as of right to all claimants who can demonstrate financial hardship.
options.B: A PCO automatically prevents any costs order being made against the claimant in judicial review proceedings.
options.C: PCOs are only available after permission has been granted.
options.D: Following R (Corner House Research) v Secretary of State for Trade and Industry [2005], a PCO may be granted where the case raises a public interest issue of general importance, the claimant has no private interest in the outcome, and the financial resources of the parties are such that failure to grant a PCO would stifle the litigation.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: R (Corner House Research) v Secretary of State for Trade and Industry [2005] EWCA Civ 192 established criteria for PCOs (now Aarhus Convention compliance also relevant for environmental cases): (i) the issues are of general public importance; (ii) the public interest requires them to be resolved; (iii) the claimant has no private interest in the outcome; (iv) having regard to the financial resources and the amount of costs, it would be fair and just to make the order. PCOs may be made at permission stage. A is wrong — not available as of right; C is wrong on timing; D overstates the effect — it caps, does not eliminate, adverse costs.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional and Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.subtopic: Parliamentary privilege — freedom of speech in Parliament
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: An MP makes statements in the House of Commons about alleged corruption by Ashworth Developments Ltd. James Ashworth wishes to bring a defamation claim based on those statements. What is the constitutional position?

options.A: The MP can be sued for defamation in respect of statements made in parliamentary proceedings, subject to qualified privilege.
options.B: The MP would be protected only if the statement was made during a formal debate, not during questions or committee proceedings.
options.C: Parliamentary privilege only protects ministers from defamation claims, not backbench MPs.
options.D: Article 9 of the Bill of Rights 1689 provides that proceedings in Parliament shall not be impeached or questioned in any court; the MP's statements in the House are absolutely privileged and cannot found a defamation action.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: Article 9 of the Bill of Rights 1689 — one of the foundational constitutional instruments — provides that 'the freedom of speech and debates or proceedings in Parliament ought not to be impeached or questioned in any court or place out of Parliament'. This confers absolute privilege on all MPs (and peers) for things said in the course of parliamentary proceedings, including questions, debates and committee hearings. No defamation action can be brought (Prebble v Television New Zealand [1994]). Options A, C and D incorrectly limit or qualify this absolute privilege.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.subject: Constitutional and Administrative Law
Constitutional & Administrative Law.subtopic: Advising public authority clients — duties and conflicts
Constitutional & Administrative Law.stem: A solicitor at Crane & Pelletier advises Wharton Borough Council on its response to a judicial review challenge brought by the Wharton Ecology Trust. The council wishes to continue a policy the solicitor believes has less than a 25% prospect of being upheld. Under the SRA Code of Conduct 2019, what is the solicitor's primary obligation?

options.A: The solicitor must advise the council to immediately concede the proceedings regardless of the council's own assessment.
options.B: The solicitor must withdraw immediately from representing the council if there is any risk the case will fail.
options.C: The solicitor's duty is solely to advance the council's chosen position regardless of its prospects.
options.D: The solicitor must give the council an honest assessment of prospects, including the risk of an adverse costs order, and advise on merits even if this is unwelcome; the solicitor cannot knowingly mislead the court about the strength of the council's case.

Constitutional & Administrative Law.explanation: The SRA Code of Conduct 2019 (Principles 2, 4 and 7 and para 6.4) requires solicitors to act with honesty and integrity, to maintain trust in the profession, and to act in clients' best interests. Paragraph 6.4 requires that solicitors do not mislead the court. The duty to the client includes honest advice on prospects (even where unwelcome), including the financial risk of adverse costs. The solicitor must not advance a case they know to be hopeless without disclosing the position candidly. Option A removes client autonomy; C prioritises client wishes over honesty; D is an overreaction — a poor-prospects case is not automatically grounds for withdrawal.
